(And using a vehicle that doesn't shield you in the least, such as a broom, as opposed to a motorcycle with fairings that guide the majority of the wind away from you) So while the bodysuit might help minimize air resistance and clothing flapping about, it might not be ideal for your witches not suffering hypothermia. Could serve as a bit of flavour, making cold climates a viable counter to witches, by significantly limiting their air time, unless they wear warm clothes, which minimize their speed... Then you could counter the counter, by having witches who specialized in just that situation and discovered magic that protects them from the cold while wearing their trademark skintight suits.

item.title: Dungeon
item.description: Die Spieler sind in einem klassischen Dungeon und sollen ein Artefakt beschaffenPeriphäre Systeme: Statuseffekte Umgebungsgefahren

item.title: Dämonenbeschwörung
item.description: Die Spieler sind Teenagerfreunde, die als Mutprobe einen Dämon beschwören wollen - leider beschwört das einen echten Dämon, der nicht freundlich gesinnt ist.Periphäre Systeme: Statuseffekte

item.title: Marktplatzschlacht
item.description: Während eines gewöhnlichen Shoppingtrips bricht auf dem Markt plötzlich ein Tumult aus, als ein paar Diebe einen Händler ausrauben und davonrennen. Die Spieler werden in der Not aufgefordert, die Diebe zu stoppenPeriphäre Systeme: Improvisierte Waffen Umgebungsgefahren

item.title: Kollosseum
item.description: Die Spieler sind Krieger in einer Arena und sollen um die Gunst auf dem Schlachtfeld kämpfen.Periphäre Systeme: Battle Kits Massenunterhaltung

item.title: Kampf in der Taverne
item.description: In einer Taverne sitzt die Gruppe Spieler, als plötzlich ein paar betrunkene Gäste anfangen die Spieler zu belästigenPeriphäre Systeme: Statuseffekte

item.title: Monsterjagd im Nebel
item.description: Die Gruppe Spieler sind Kryptologen, welche in einem Nebeligen Sumpf ein Monster suchen - nur um herauszufinden, dass es wirklich existiertPeriphäre Systeme: ???

item.title: Narrative - Mortal Engines
item.description: Mortal Engines is set in a post-apocalyptic world, where humanity has rebuilt its cities to traverse the unforgiving lands. No connecting roads between cities allow motorized vehicles to find their way around the outskirts, instead most intercity travel happens on lighter-than-air floating airships. The game of Mortal Engines: XXXXXX is about resourcefulness, clever solutioms to problems, and preplanning. This game throws the players into the world, with the game master acting as their five senses. They each embody one or more characters to roam the lands of the hunting grounds, the static settlements of the Anti-Traction-League, or any other place on earth, to trade goods with witting salespeople, to uncover dark secrets within conspiracies in the fight for survival between the cities. The game masters role is to provide an interactive world for the players that can be changed as they desire, they will take note of the players actions and let the world react accordingly. The players role is to be curious about the world and story the game master is presenting, and to interact with elements of the world in creative ways to find solutions to challenges the game master prepares for the player characters to overcome.

item.title: Tempel
item.description: Die Spieler betreten einen Tempel, als plötzlich die dekorativen Statuen zum Leben erwachen.Periphäre Systeme: Umgebungsgefahren Fallen

item.title: Tanzbühne
item.description: Die Gruppe wird in Paare aufgeteilt, jedes Paar soll kooperativ in einem Tanzwettbewerb die meisten Punkte erzielen. Dann wird die Bühne gestürmt, doch dank der Security, welche das ganze kaschieren will, denkt die Menge, dass das zur Show gehört. Jetzt müssen die Spieler tanzend die Eindringlinge besiegen.Periphäre Systeme: Kooperative Angriffe Massenunterhaltung

item.title: Schiffsgefecht
item.description: Die Spieler bemannen ein oder zwei kleine Schiffe, um ihren Heimathafen zu verteidigen.Periphäre Systeme: Größenskalierung von Verletzungen

item.title: Injury Slots Battle Engine
item.description:
Anstelle dass jeder Charakter beliebig viele Verletzungen tragen kann, solange der Pool an D6er nur groß genug ist, basiert das Steckplatzsystem auf der Annahme, dass jeder Charakter irgendwann den Geist aufgibt. Jeder Charakter kann, abhängig von den Attributen Stärke und Reflexe, je eine gewisse Anzahl an Verletzungen pro Schweregrad aushalten, bevor dieser Charakter aus dem Spiel fällt. Diese Plätze heißen Verletzungsplätze.
Die Anzahl an Verletzungsplätzen ist nur zu Beginn eines neuen Kapitels veränderlich. Zu diesem Zeitpunkt summiert der Spieler die Attributspunkte von Stärke und Reflexe, addiert 3 zu diesem Wert hinzu und erhält eine einzelne Punktzahl. Anhand dieser Punktzahl werden nach folgender Metrik die Verletzungsplätze bestimmt:
Für jeden Punkt kann der Charakter eine Schramme ertragen.
Für jeden zweiten Punkt kann der Charakter eine Wunde ertragen.
Für jeden dritten Punkt kann der Charakter ein Trauma ertragen, sofern der Verletzungslevel nicht mehr als 4 beträgt. Im Laufe des Spiels kommt es mit großer Wahrscheinlichkeit vor, dass ein Charakter in einer gefährlichen Situation landet und verletzt wird. Der Verletzungslevel wird wie gewohnt bestimmt. Abhängig von diesem Level entsteht ein Schweregrad für diese Verletzung, welche in einen passenden Verletzungsplatz gesetzt werden muss. Dabei darf der Platz nicht von geringerer Schwere sein als die Verletzung selbst. Ein Platz kann nur eine Verletzung gleichzeitig tragen. Der Verletzungslevel wird in den Kasten unter dem Namen der Verletzung eingetragen.
Sollte von einem Schweregrad kein freier Platz zur Verfügung stehen, muss diese Verletzung in den nächstgrößeren Schweregrad eingetragen werden. Sollte kein ausreichend schwerer Platz mehr frei sein, ist der Charakter niedergestreckt und steht nun vor Des Todes Tür - Variante.Es ist möglich, dass Charaktere des Spiels resistent oder verwundbar gegenüber einem bestimmten Schadenstypen sind.
Sollte ein Charakter resistent gegen einen Schadenstypen sein, wird die Verletzung vor Platzierung auf die nächstgeringere Schwere heruntergestuft. Eine nicht modifizierte Schramme wird annulliert.
Sollte ein Charakter verwundbar durch einen Schadenstypen sein, wird die Verletzung vor Platzierung auf die nächsthöhere Schwere hochgestuft. Ein nicht modifiziertes Trauma geht direkt in Des Todes Tür - Variante über.Sobald ein Charakter niedergestreckt wurde, hat dieser noch 2 Runden zu leben. Während des eigenen Zugs kann ein Charakter entweder körperlich leichte Aktivitäten wie leise Reden oder einen Arm bewegen. Alternativ kann der Charakter auch seine Kraft sammeln und darauf hoffen, dass ein Schicksalswurf ihn wieder ins Spiel bringen kann, wenn auch beeinträchtigt. Dazu wirft der Spieler einen D20 und vergleicht das Ergebnis mit einem vom Erzähler gesetzten Wert, welcher als Barriere zum Wiedereinstieg wirkt. Liegt das Ergebnis mit dem Wert gleich, ist der Charakter vor dem unmittelbaren Tod bewahrt, aber für den Rest der aktuellen Begegnung nicht in der Lage, jemanden anzugreifen oder die gleiche Aktion erneut auszuführen, die erst zuvor zu der Nahtoderfahrung geführt hat. Außerhalb des eigenen Zuges dürfen andere Charaktere dem niedergestreckten Charakter zur Hilfe eilen, Wunden versorgen und so die Chancen auf einen Wiedereinstieg erhöhen. Es wird empfohlen, die medizinische Versorgung als kollaborative Probe durchzuführen.
Verletzungen verschiedenen Schweregrades heilen auf unterschiedliche Weise. Schrammen können auf natürlichem Wege vollständig verheilen, alles andere benötigt angemessene medizinische Ausrüstung.Ein mal pro Kapitel kann der Erzähler verkünden, dass nun natürliche Heilung einsetzt. An diesem Punkt darf jeder Spieler eine beliebige Verletzung aussuchen, und den Wert um 1 verringern.
Sollte eine Schramme dadurch auf 0 reduziert werden, so wird diese Verletzung komplett entfernt und der Verletzungsplatz steht wieder frei.
Sollte eine Wunde oder ein Trauma gewählt werden, darf auch der Wert um 1 reduziert werden, allerdings bildet dies eine permanente Narbe. Diese Verletzung ist nun weder durch natürliche Heilung noch durch medizinische Behandlung weiter heilbar. Eine natürlich verheilte Verletzung stellt keinen negativen Effekt für den Charakter dar ähnlich einer behandelten Verletzung. Wann es für die Geschichte passt, können Charaktere in der Lage sein, Verletzungen medizinisch versorgen zu lassen. Dieser Prozess findet unabhängig von natürlicher Heilung statt. Charaktere dürfen zu diesen Gelegenheiten beliebige noch nicht behandelte oder verheilte Verletzungen behandeln lassen. Jede Verletzung muss dabei einzeln behandelt werden. Diese Behandlung setzt den Zugang zu angemessenen medizinischen Ressourcen voraus. Mit ausreichendem medizinischen Wissen und Ressourcen ist es für einen Charakter möglich, eine Behandlung durchzuführen. Dies darf als kollaborative Probe mit anderen Charakteren erfolgen, wobei alle teilnehmenden Charaktere ohne ausreichendes medizinisches Wissen einen Malus von -2 Erfolgen automatisch mitbringen. Der Challenge Level für diese Probe ist der Verletzungslevel. Die gewählte Verletzung wird um den Erfolgslevel dieser Probe reduziert, sollte die Probe fehlschlagen, verschlimmert sich die Verletzung. Die behandelte Verletzung kann nach der Behandlung bis zur nächsten natürlichen Heilung nicht erneut behandelt werden.Umgebungsgefahren sind lokale Änderungen der Umgebung, welche negative Auswirkungen auf Charaktere haben können. Jede Umgebungsgefahr bildet dabei einen Bereich, innerhalb dessen die Gefahr besteht, von den Auswirkungen betroffen zu sein. Ein Aufenthalt in der Gefahrenzone birgt ein konstantes Risiko, den Auswirkungen zu erliegen. Sich in eine Gefahrenzone zu beheben resultiert in einer Probe auf einen zur Gefahr passenden Core-Skill, der vom Erzähler angegeben wird. Die Umgebungsgefahr gibt an, wie viele narrative Erfolge benötigt werden, um die Probe für eine gewisse Zeit zu bestehen, wie nach dieser Zeit erneut die Probe absolviert werden muss, und durch welche Aktionen oder Gegenstände diese Probe erleichtert oder umgangen werden kann.Beispiele Feuer Bereich: Boden, Kreis 5m Zusätzliche Erschwerungen:
Wahrnehmung -1 Verfassung CL3: Den Flammen trotzen Nachteil durch: Trockene und leicht entflammbare getragene Gegenstände Erfolg Der Charakter wird von den bestehenden Flammen für VL Aktionen nicht in Brand gesetzt. Fehlschlag Der Charakter fängt Feuer und brennt. Mit jeder Aktion im brennenden Zustand erhöht sich die Verletzung Verbrennung ILx um 1IL. Rauchschwaden Bereich: Zylinder, Radius 5m Zusätzliche Erschwerungen:
Perception -1 Willpower CL3: Das Giftgas nicht einatmen Erleichtert durch: Luftfilter Eine Art Luftfilter vor den Mund zu halten, erleichtert diese Probe um eine Anzahl NaS, je nach Effektivität des Filters: Hand vor Mund und Nase: 1NaS, 1 Hand ist verhindert Kleidung: 1NaS Nasse Kleidung: 2NaS Atemfiltermaske: 3NaS Luftdichter Helm Ein Luftdichter Helm lässt diese Probe entfallen, solange dieser Helm funktionsfähig ist. Erfolg Der Charakter schafft es, die Luft für VL Aktionen anzuhalten. Anschließend muss die Probe erneut absolviert werden. Fehlschlag Der Charakter atmet giftige Rauchgase ein. Verletzung: Rauchvergiftung | IL2 wird hinzugefügt. Solange diese Verletzung besteht, hustet der Charakter unkontrolliert. Während die meisten Kämpfe eher so ausgetragen werden, dass an Ende gewinnt, wer eher genug gegnerische Charaktere ausgeschaltet hat, kann es auch vorkommen, dass die Art, wie Gegner ausgeschaltet werden, sogar noch relevanter ist als wer schneller ist. Beispielsweise könnte in einer Arena ein Kampf vor Zuschauern ausgetragen werden, die es zu unterhalten gilt. An dieser Stelle kommen Stilpunkte ins Spiel. Stilpunkte sind ein abstraktes Mittel um festzuhalten, wie elegant ein Charakter die eigenen Aktionen miteinander verknüpft. Wird ein Kampf nach Stilpunkten entschieden, zählen nicht unbedingt die effektivsten Angriffe, sondern die am aufregendsten aussehenden. Jede Aktion erzeugt eine vom Erzähler spontan gesetzte Anzahl an Stilpunkten. Stilpunkte sind ein relativer Wert. Es gibt grundsätzlich keine obere Grenze beim Bewerten von Aktionen, es wird jedoch der Einfachheit halber empfohlen, kleinere Zahlen zu nehmen. Verschiedene Faktoren spielen in die stilvolle Bewertung einer Aktion ein: die Wünsche und die Aufmerksamkeit des Publikums (Wünsche erfüllen = mehr Stilpunkte) die Komplexität und Präsentation der Aktion (mehr Komplexität = mehr Stilpunkte) wiederholende Aktionen oder Situationen (Wiederholungen = weniger Stilpunkte) die Wahrscheinlichkeit für das Wiederherstellen einer vergangenen Situation für den aktiven Charakter (unwahrscheinlicher = mehr Stilpunkte) Beispiel Die beiden Wrestler Antonio und Bridar sind in einem Ring. Die Zuschauerränge sind gut gefüllt. Antonio greift zuerst an und will Bridar mit einer Rempelattacke umrennen. Das gelingt, und so verdient Antonio ein paar Stilpunkte. In der nächsten Runde plant er das gleiche zu tun. Da er genau die gleiche Aktion gerade erst durchgeführt hat, verdient er beim nächsten Mal weniger Stilpunkte dafür. Beispiel Das Wrestlingduell zwischen Antonio one Bridar läuft schon deutlich länger, ohne dass eine großartige Veränderung des Status Quo eingezogen ist. Stattdessen ist nur zu sehen, wie zwei fast nackte Männer sich gegenseitig in den Würgegriff nehmen und zu Boden werfen. Nach einigen Runden dauert es für die Zuschauer zu lange, und viele wenden sich ab. Das verdient beiden Wrestlern deutlich weniger Stilpunkte im weiteren Verlauf des Kampfes. Beispiel Im Kampf wird Bridar von Antonio angegriffen. Da Bridar eine höhere Initiative hat als sein Gegner, darf er auf den Angriff reagieren. Bridar entscheidet sich dazu, dem Angriff auszuweichen und zu kontern. Wäre Bridar nur ausgewichen, würde ihm das weniger Stilpunkte einbringen, als mit einem Konter. Abhängig von der Komplexität des beschriebenen Konters und wie erfolgreich Bridar mit diesem ist, erhält er entsprechend viele Stilpunkte. Beispiel Die beiden Wrestler Bridar und Antonio wurden zu einem blutigen Kampf in eine Arena geladen. Das Publikum erwartet fliegende Zähne, fiese Tricks und brutale Gewalttaten. Solange die beiden Wrestler nicht diesen Erwartungen nachkommen, verdienen sie weniger Stilpunkte.

item.title: Design Ideas
item.description: Different difficulty System 5 difficulty tiers (trivial, easy, moderate, hard, impossible) trivial auto succeed easy auto surge moderate 2dx hard auto stall impossible auto fail (make it clear beforehand) ap => Σ(2dx) Aided Skill Checks if an interaction falls under the domain of a specific skill but the action of that interaction can be supplemented by another skill, that check can be aided by the supplement skill. If this is the case, a player may roll the skill check as normal but also apply a +1 to the result, if the character has a positive amount of skill points allocated in the supplemental skill. Help Action

item.title: 5D
item.description:
Introduction and game preparationThis roleplaying game is a Setting-agnostic cooperative storytelling System that is played by at least 2 people. The game can be adapted to fit a multitude of different Settings. In a game of 5D, Players come together to collectively tell a Story within an agreed upon Setting using Roleplaying as the main driver behind Character choices, while Outcomes of Actions with uncertain Results are determined mostly through Dice Rolls. One Player known as the Narrator describes the World of the Story and important elements in the surroundings of the Player Characters, which are controlled by the other Players. Each Player may take control over one or more Characters, with any left over Characters being taken over by the Narrator.
Every Player needs to have access to two full sets of standard polymetric Dice, including the D4, D6, D8, D10, D12, D20 and D%). Furthermore, all Players should have read and understood the rules that are relevant to their Role, including any Setting specific rules which can alter the game.
Setting specific preparation Every Setting follows the core rules laid out in this document, but may add their specific Mechanics and Subsystems to enhance or alternate the experience. In case a Setting requires more preparation than is listed here, the Setting is listing the preparation steps needed separately in its own section.
After all Players can agree on a specific type of Story, they collectively decide who should be the Narrator, the rest will continue asCharacters. Every Player may take control over one or more Characters within the story to dictate those Characters Actions. These picked Characters are called Player Characters. The Narrator is a special Role among the Players. They are responsible for describing the World the Story takes place in and the Actions performed by the Player Characters and how they affect the environment.
In principal, every Player should begin the game with a certain goal in mind, something they wish to achieve during this game. This goal helps inform the narrator on where to take the Story.
Once the bearer of the Narrator Role has been decided, the Narrator can begin the game preparation.
Preparing a game of 5D is much like planning out certain events of a Story. A Story is typically structured into one or more Arcs, each divided into Chapters, which in turn can be divided into one or more Scenes.
When starting preparation of a new game of 5D, the narrator need only to pick a Chapter goal, as this is usually the amount of Story that a game can provide within a single session. This Chapter goal is informed by the overarching currently active Narrative and the major plot points who are driving the Story forward. The Narrator is also advised to take the Players goals into consideration at this point, to increase Player engagement. The goal can be newly created or a preexisting one from a previous adventure.
After the goal is defined, next up the Narrator should assess the current situation of the main party, where they are located and what they are planning to do. This is the point where game preparation can differ wildly depending on whether the Players are starting a new Story or continuing a previous adventure.
If the Players continue a previous adventure, the narrator is advised to revisit the scenes of the last game, to inform themselves of where the Players left off. This takes off a lot of the prep work on this step required, as it already establishes who the Characters are, how they met and what they each want to accomplish in the story at this point. Should the Players start a new Story, they have to collectively come up with all the answers to this from scratch. It is helpful to answer these questions in order to begin a new Story: Who is each character? What do they have in common? What differentiates them? How did they first meet?
The Narrator may want to add a few different options in order to achieve the goal the Players have set out to accomplish, which ideally cover different play styles to ensure Players have different viable choices to pick their favorite path from. Finally, to map out the beats of the Story, the Narrator should prepare a few core scenes that will be key in achieving the goal through one or more options.
Finally, when the goal is known and the path there roughly mapped out, the Narrator can connect back with the Players and figure out an opening Scene.
By the nature of the game, 5D is best used for telling a Story over one or more sessions.
Like any Story, its structure can be broken down into Arcs, each being made up of Chapters, which in turn can be broken down even further into Scenes.
All of these Story chunks can be as large or small as the Narrator deems fit. They can be a tiny throw-away line skipping a whole Chapter, or they could use a few hours on a single Scene.Skill Checks
Skill Checks are a tool to determine the Outcome of any interaction with not predetermined Results. They can be used whenever a Character attempts something and the Outcome is not fully known.
A Skill Check always follows a simple structure: First, the Narrator determines the Skill to be used and the Difficulty of the task. Then, the Player rolls the required Dice and sums up the Result, using any applicable Modifiers. Finally, the Narrator and the Player work together to interpret the Result into an Outcome that fits within the Narrative of the game.
In order for a Character to see how well they do on a given Skill Check, the Narrator has to set the Difficulty. This Difficulty is a numeric value ranging from 0 to 5 that increases linearly with rising difficulty of the task. The name of that numerical value indicates the intended Difficulty of that Skill Check for an average Character of that Lineage within the Setting.
Example Barbara wants to pick a lock. Because the lock is quite old and posesses a rather simple mechanism, the Narrator sets this Skill Check as an Uncommon Grace task.
Example Grimsby is trying to jump up a tall ledge. The narrator sets the Skill Check as a Challenging Might task. Since Grimsby is a kangaroo and not a human, he gets to reduce the Difficulty by 1 tier for jumping related actions as part of his Lineage. Thus, Grimsby has to succeed at an Uncommon task rather than a Challenging one to succeed in this Action.
Once the Narrator announces the Skill used for the task, the Player can pick up the required dice to roll in the check depending on the Characters training level in that Skill:
Example Barbara tries to pick the lock. Her Grace is at +1, giving her access to a 2D8 Dice Pool.
Example Grimsby has 2 Skill Points in Might, allowing him to roll 2D10 on his attempt to jump up the ledge.
Depending on the task at hand, the Difficulty can vary. Oftentimes, the task does not exactly match the Characters Skill level perfectly, which offsets the two values.
Depending on the constellation of these two variable tiers, different Skill Check Modifiers apply automatically:
Apart from outright failing or succeeding a task, two more types of Modifiers exist that can change the Outcome of a Skill Check: Momentum and Static Modifiers.
Momentum
Rolling with Momentum means rolling the same Skill Check twice, and taking the higher or lower Result, depending on whether the Character is Surging (higher result) or Stalling (lower result).
A Character is Surging once at least one active effect grants them Surge, but no Stalling effect is applied.
Likewise, a Character is Stalling once at least one active effect applies Stall to them, but no Surging effect is granted.
Should a Character have any combination of at least one Surging and at least one Stalling effect applied to them, they cancel each other out and the Character does not gain any of these effects.
Static Modifiers
Static Modifiers are a way to adjust the Result of a roll and adding a small number to it, be it positive or negative.
After the Dice Roll (including a potential Momentum roll) is made, the player adds all applicable Static Modifiers to the rolled number, forming a final Result.
Example Barbara wishes to pick a lock with a simple mechanism. The Narrator decided that this is an uncommon task. Barbaras Skill Level is 1, which matches the task Difficulty. She thus has to roll the check once.
Example Barbara tries to pick an old lock using her lockpicking equipment. This picklock grants her a simple +1 on lockpicking Actions. Barbara uses the 2D8 from her Skill and rolls a 10. The picklocks Static Modifier boosts her result to 11.
Example Grimsby the kangaroo wants to jump up a tall ledge. The Narrator set this as an challenging task, but Grimsby has a trait that lets him reduce the Difficulty of jumping tasks by 1, pushing it down to to uncommon Difficulty. Grimsbys Might is 2, meaning it is 1 higher than the Difficulty of the task. This grants Grimsby Surge Momentum when attempting to perform this Action.
Example Grimsby is attempting to jump up a ledge. Before jumping off, he takes a running start. The Narrator rules that this gives Grimsby a Surge Momentum, but because he already has that effect applied through performing an Action of 1 less Difficulty, this second Surge gets discarded. His first roll from 2D10 is a 5, which is not enough to succeed. His second Roll results in a 7. Not much better, but enough to succeed at the task.
Looking up the Result in the table above allows for interpretation of the Outcome. In this step, the Player and Narrator have to cooperate to tell a fitting and narratively satisfying Outcome for this task.
Example Barbara rolled an 11 when trying to lockpick an old lock, resulting in a Simple Success. Barbara is able to pick the lock easily without any guard nearby being alerted.
Example Grimsby achieved a 7 when trying to jump up the ledge. This barely reaches the a Complicated Success. The Player offers to take an Injury as Complication from misjudging the jump. The Glossary > Narrator describes how Grimsby takes a running start, jumps off and is able to reach the ledges height, but he jumped too early and now hangs on the edge of the ledge with his paws. The hard jolt on his arms strains the muscles and bones, and Grimsby has received a Strain through this Glossary > Action.
When a Character is faced with a challenge they are not likely to succeed at, the Player may decide to utilize a fitting Approach. Approaches are a way to boost a Character chances of success against a Glossary > Skill Check that otherwise would result in failure. The Approach does need to be approved by the narrator, as nothing narratively nonsensical should be incorporated into the Story.
When a new Approach is created, it is noted down on the Character Sheet. Upon first use, due to being something the Character is not yet trained in, this Approach can be used only with a Static Modifier of -2. This modifier does not apply on any subsequent uses of this Approach.
Example A rogue named Syla needs to convince a guard that they were not the one who tried to pickpocket them. The Narrator tells the Player controlling Syla that this is a challenging Charms check. As Syla is terrible in their persuasion, the player asks the Narrator if they could try to distract the guard using their Grace Skill, by throwing a rock against a nearby object unseen as if another Character just ran away. The Narrator allows this creative thinking as an Approach, as this still needs some convincing for the guard to believe there was another person there. So, Sylas Player now may use one Charms die and one Grace die in order to roll this Action. Syla has amazing Grace, granting the Player a D12 instead of the second D4 from a raw Charms check. As this is a new Approach, the Player makes this check but has to roll with a -2 Static Modifier. The two Dice result in a 11 and a 4, which sums up to 15. Subtracting the -2 gives a 13, which is enough for a Simple Success. Syla manages to distract the guard and convince them of there being another person, which gives them a tiny opportunity to flee from the scene.
It is possible to help out other Characters through the use of a Link Approach. The helping character rolls and Approach with the Link Skill and the Skill Checks required Skill with the Difficulty being 1 less than the original Skill Checks. The Outcome is a Static Modifier on the original Skill Check , whose value is determined through this table:
When a longer process gets started by one or more Characters, the Narrator may introduce a Progress Clock. These devices are a visual tool for showing long term progress towards some abstract goal.
Progress Clocks are usually divided into a number of segments that get filled as Actions related to that Clocks goal succeed or fail. Anytime a segment gets filled with a positive or negative value depending on success or failure, the narrator can have a positive or negative effect occur that will influence the current situation.
Most Progress Clocks follow this format:
An overall goal: This is the name of the Progress Clock. It informs about the target being pursued with this Clock. A number of segments: This is the number of actions that have to be performed at most in relation to the goal. At latest when the last segment gets filled in, the overall success on the goal should be evaluated. Time per segment: This number tells how long completing a single segment takes for a single character.
A number of successes: This is the threshold of success, at which the goal is generally considered to have a positive turnout. The Characters working on this Progress Clocks must succeed at least this many times to win this Progress Clocks.
A number of failures: This is the threshold of failure, at which the goal is generally considered to have a negative turnout. The Characters working on this Progress Clocks must not reach this number of failed actions on the goal to avoid the negative consequences. The number of successes and number of failures sum up to the number of segments plus 1.
Creating a Progress Clock A typical Progress Clock could look like this: Decypher the encoded letter: 8*1h - 6S/3F
This Progress Clock defines the overall goal to be the decoding of a script. The Clock has 8 segments and the timeframe for each segment is 1 hour, meaning this task will take 8 hours in total to complete. The number of successes is 6 and the number of failures is 3, this means that the characters must achieve at least 6 successes before they reach 3 failures in order to succeed at decyphering this letter.
For a Character to advance an existing Progress Clock, the character must perform an Action whose outcome aligns with the Clocks goal. When the Character does that, they roll a normal Skill Check to see whether they advance the goal or not: On a simple success or failure, the result is entered into the clock and the clock advances by one segment. Critical result variants double the number of successes/failures written on the clock and advance the clock by double the amount.
It is possible for a Character to help out another Character that is currently working on a Progress Clock by rolling a Link Approach with the substituting Skill being the one used by the Character directly working on the Progress Clock. Should the help consist of less direct actions, for example gathering appropriate materials, an appropriate Skill is to be used instead.
Once the worked on Clock has been filled out completely, the task is completed. How well the Characters did on the task and whether the goal has been accomplished depends on the numbers of successes and failures the Characters have added to the Clock:
If the number of required successes has been reached before the number of failures was, the Characters are successful in their task.
If the number of maximum failures has been reached before the number of successes was, the Characters fail in their task.
If the number of required successes has been surpassed byat least half of the number of failures (rounded down), the Characters succeed critically.
If the number of maximum failures has been surpassed by at least half of the number of successes (rounded down), the Characters fail critically.
Succeeding a Progress Clock The party is tasked with decyphering an encrypted letter. The task can be worked on in 1-hour segments and will take about 8 hours of work. The smartest two Characters share the workload and help each other out to advance the goal faster. After just 4 hours of their combined effort they are able to share their results with the rest of the party. The segments of the Progress Clock are filled in as follows: S-S-S-F-S-S-S-S. This sums up to 1 failure and 7 successes. As they have reached the required number of successes, they do succeed at the task and are able to read the decyphered letter. Even better yet, because they surpassed the number of successes by half of the number of failures rounded down (3/2 = 1.5 -> 1), they succeed critically, thus are able to extract more info out of the letter than expected.
Failing a Progress Clock The party is tasked with decyphering an encrypted letter. The task can be worked on in 1-hour segments and will take about 8 hours of work. The smartest two Characters share the workload and help each other out to advance the goal faster. After just 4 hours of their combined effort they are able to share their results with the rest of the party. The segments of the Progress Clock are filled in as follows: F-F-S-S-F-F-S-F. This sums up to 3 successes and 5 failures. As they have reached the maximum number of failures, they fail at the task and are not able to read the decyphered letter. Even worse yet, because they surpassed the number of failures by half of the number of successes rounded down (6/2 = 3), they fail critically. The party not only does not get the correct letter content, they manage to somehow misinterpret the whole letter and assume different information based on the wrong results. ConflictsVitalityEquipmentDuring skirmishes, carrying weapons and wearing armor has oftentimes proven effective to amplify ones chances of success for reaching their goal, be it defeating other characters, invading a base, or simply surviving a battle. In 5D, weapons and armor are pieces of equipment that a character has in their inventory.Weapons are the main way of increasing both the reach of an attack damage output of said attack. To track weapons effectively and efficiently, they all fall into this scheme: Name: every weapon has an identifier. By default it is a descriptor of the weapon, eg "Boxing Gloxes", but players are free to rename weapons however they like, as long as it is clear what weapon they are talking about. Type (Skill): every weapon that is used to attack relies on some form of wielder skill. For melee weapons, this is usually Might, ranged weapons usually use Agility. Advanced weapons that don't require manual input typically require Wits, and magical weapons might rely on Charms. Reach: the effective range the weapon is meant to be used in. If the target is too close, the attack automatically stalls. If the target is too far and the weapon is a melee weapon, the attack can not hit. If the target is too far and the weapon is a ranged weapon, the attack automatically stalls. By default, there are 5 different ranges: Grapple, Melee, Long, Far, and Artillery. Miniature Fights only: Which range the distance between a character and the target falls under is determined by these measurement methods: If the distance between the two characters is less than the narrators pinky fingers width, the range is Grapple. If the distance between the two characters is less than the narrators index fingers and pinky fingers combined, the range is Melee. If the distance between the two characters is less than the distance from the narrators index pinky finger to the index finger, the range is Long. If the distance between the two characters is less than the narrators maximum span of a single hand, the range is Far. Anything outside that distance falls under Artillery. Grip: most weapons need to be held in some form. Every weapon lists what kind of grip is necessary to use it properly. Improper grip can be used, if narratively justifiable, but results in a stall. By default, there are 3 different grips: One Handed, Two Handed and Twinned. Damage Types: every attack uses some sort of damage type. Resistances and Vulnerabilities against these damage types change the injury severity on the target. By default, there are 3 different damage types: Blunt, Cut, Pierce Extra Traits: any important additional info about the weapon gets noted down here. Example on a Twinned Grip Sable: "Twin Blades: when used with a second Theft Blade and the character has a vocation for using weapons, attacks automatically surge". While weapons can prove useful to afflict stronger injuries on an opponent, armor helps protecting against too severe injuries. Armor comes in three coverage tiers, each protecting more than the previous one: half armor, when worn, will cover the bulk of the wearer. full armor, when worn, will cover the bulk and appendages of the wearer but decrease dodging by -2 closed armor is made up of full armor and an ace piece, which covers the wearers weak point but decreases dodging by -4. Along with these three cover tiers, armor comes in different strength levels: basic armor adds a static modifier of +2 for blocking. refined armor adds a static modifier of +4 for blocking. fortified armor adds a static modifier of +6 for blocking. Character SheetCharacter CreationA character within 5Dice can be created within minutes of having a good idea. Using the following process you can manifest your dream easily.It all starts with a simple idea, known as the pitch. A pitch is a narrative hook that draws the attention of the audience into the pitched idea by providing an interesting twist to an otherwise potentially mundane thought. The pitch should be no longer than two sentences. From this pitch a concept can be formed. The concept is more concrete than the pitch in terms of how that character fits into the world, but it is not as constricting as choosing a city to be born in. This concept is the backbone of your character design, and on a narrative level as many elements of the character creation process as possible should conform to support this core idea.After finding a concept, it is time to fill in the backstory of the character. The backstory tells about a characters past, and the reasons why they are in their current position. It is recommended to keep the backstory simple and written in bullet points for easier reference.With the backstory you also know where the character stands in their own life. Now you can fill in the characters identity, which consists of their name, age, origin, and characteristics. The character origin refers to the place they were born. Characteristics mean special features of the character that are minor tweaks to the appearance or behavior that can be used to gain rust.After the backstory it is time for the goals and aspirations. You know where the character came from, now you need to decide on where they want to go. A character can have a single goal or multiple goals. It is recommended to keep the list of goals as short as possible. Goals represent achievements that are to the character worth pursuing for their intrinsic values rather for external motivation.A characters beliefs guide the character throughout the world with a filter of morale through which the character forms a certain world view, which in turn shapes their morals. From a characters origin their beliefs can be inferred, and the initial moral is copied from the kind of society they are a part of.After the narrative part of the character is created, the gameplay section can be filled in. Using the notes from the previously filled areas, decide on how to spend the characters skill points. On tier 1, a character has 2 skill points, and on tier 2 a character has 4 skill points. All core skills of any character start at 0 skill points, which translates into a D6. Skill points are used on skills to increase the dice value by 2 (to a maximum of 3 with a resulting D12), or a skill point may be gained by reducing one D6 to a D4.The second part of the abilities are the professions. Every character must additionally have at least a major profession and may have a minor profession at the cost of 1 skill point. The major profession is their way of earning their daily bread, be it by playing music for others, accounting finances, robbing people on the streets or hunting animals in the wild. The minor profession can be either a hobby, a side project that has nothing to do with the major profession, or it could even be a former job that still left some knowledge. Professions are used during skill checks where applicable major professions grant a highroll to the result and applicable minor professions add +2 to the result.A characters starting equipment heavily depends on a multitude of factors, like the origin, the characters upbringing, their backstory, the professions, or their morals. It is best practice to work with the game master on this point, as the starting equipment can vary wildly between characters.Error accessing selected text: TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'file')

item.title: Crafts
item.description: Characters are able to create new objects from available resources and materials. Crafting can be useful to invent new weapons on the fly, forge evidence or cook food. Even medical surgery can be tracked with this system.To begin crafting, characters need knowledge over what they are trying to craft. This can be almost anything that takes some time to create, be it a hearty meal, or writing a computer program, or composing a heartwarming melody specifically for an audience made up of nobles.When the product is clear, the narrator creates a progress clock that the crafting character has to complete. The number of segments represents the amount of work that goes into the crafting process, and the number of successes determines how well the character has to perform at the craft to succeed as a whole. While other progress clocks use a single threshold to determine success and failure for this task, crafting can take up to four different outcomes all depending on the values for these two variables. Successes on each segment are determined based on normal skill checks for this kind of task, eg Agility for making a weapon, Charms for painting a picture, Wits for analyzing a super complex virus. Characters are able to use approaches here if narratively justifiable.To perform crafts, characters have to invest time. This time is most often found in downtime, during which characters are free to do anything they wish to do as long as they are uninterrupted. The total time needed to finish a craft is the number of segments multiplied by the amount of time per segment. Characters are free to choose how much of their downtime they want to spend on the craft. Whenever the character performs the required skill check on the craft, the player fills the next segments in the progress clock based on the skill checks outcome: Critical failure adds two failures simple failure adds one failure complicated success does nothing and just loses time simple success adds one success critical success adds two successes. It is possible to help out other characters when they craft something. To help a crafting character, the character has to have knowledge over the goal. When that is given, the helping character can perform a Synergy approach with the crafts required skill. This mainly reduces the time it takes to complete a craft, but is an equally great opportunity to roleplay the characters interaction on a specific topic.After the craft has been finished, i.e. the progress clock is fully filled out, the completing player counts the number of successes on the progress clock. If the success threshold has been reached, the craft was successful and the character get exactly what they set out to do, otherwise it failed and they get some other thing. If the craft has been completed with at least three more successes than required, the craft was a critical success and the character gets an item that does not only do what they wanted it to, but the narrator adds some extra on top. If the craft has been completed with 3 or more successes less than required, the craft failed critically. Not only does the character not achieve what they intended to, but they also get some other heavy drawback on it, e.g. the materials are not recoverable, or the item destroys itself.

item.title: Commerce
item.description: During their ventures, characters sometimes need some form of currency to purchase certain items from other characters. These currencies are not tracked completely, but via a commerce system.Every character posesses a purse. In this purse, all their money is stored. A purse can hold any amount of money of any currency introduced. A purse is a physical item that can be stolen by others. The purse consists of a list of currencies with a number ranging from 0 to 8. This number indicates the characters relative wealth in that currency. Item prices are measured in this relative value as well.The relative values for characters are: 1: Pauper 2: Commoner, Paycheck-to-Paycheck-Liver 3: Tradesperson- Craftsperson 4: Accomplished Career, Small Business Owner 5: Wealthy, Successful Merchant, Minor Nobility 6: CEO, Nobility 7: Magnate, Tycoon, High Nobility 8: King / Queen, Head of Megacorporation Important It is important to note that these levels of commerce increase in wealth exponentially instead of linearly. Tip The commerce level of a character is only representing the amount of currency that player has on them, it does not include personal assets or wares to be sold. Examples for relative item values are: 0: A basic meal, worn down clothes, a beer, a magazine 1: Basic food for a day, a night at an inn, a new item of clothing, a bottle of high value alcoholics 2: An extravagant night out, a basic weapon, common livestock, full camping gear, simple transport 3: A well-crafted weapon, a decent horse/car/vehicle, simple armor, a small living space 4: A very fancy horse/car/vehicle, a nice home, an extravagant weapon, full plate armor, hired bodyguard 5: A mansion, luxury transport, estate, a personal guard or security forces, established business 6: A castle/skyscraper, enterprise level company, large army, a town, a significant military asset 7: A metropole, armada, small country 8: Kingdom, Nation Important Likewise with character commerce levels, item values increase exponentially instead of linearly. Example James is a well-off buseness man. He inherited a small fortune from his grandparents. In sum, this elevates his commerce level to tier 5. Should James acquire enough money to buy a new sportscar, this would only affect his commerce after a couple times over, due to exponential increases in wealth status. When a character decides to engage in trading, this usually results in either a bargain, where both parties agree on swapping assets with no money involved, or a trade. A trade is defined by one or more characters purchasing one or more items of some value from one or more other characters.The commerce levels of the buyer and seller are affected as follows: A character buying an item of a value higher than their own commerce level is not possible, they can not afford it. A character buying an item of a value equal to their own commerce level reduces their own commerce level by 2 tiers. A character buying an item of a value one tier less than their own commerce level reduces their own commerce level by 1 tier. A character buying an item of a value at least two tiers less than their own commerce level does not reduce their own commerce level. A character selling an item of a value higher than their own commerce level increases their own commerce level to that item values commerce level. A character selling an item of a value queal to their own commerce level increases their commerce level by 1 tier. A character selling an item of a value less than their own commerce level does not increase their own commerce level. Example James has wants to acquire the new sportscar he layed eyes on for a while now. His current commerce level of 5 matches the sportscars commerce value. On purchase James loses 2 commerce levels, bumping his fluid monetary value down to tier 3. This commerce level will take a while to recover to its normal level. Example James has has enough of his new sportscar. He wants to sell it. As he is currently commerce level 3, selling the sportscar will move it up to 5 again. When trying to make a deal with another character, a character can always try to increase their profits by haggling. This is resolved as three chained conflicts, with each character rolling a choice of INT or CHA each time. The character that rolled the higher result more gets to shift the items commerce value by one tier in a direction of their choosing.Example James tries to haggle during the sell of his sportscar. He rolls his charisma, which gives him a D10. His trade partner rolls intelligence, which gives them a D8. James rolls a 3, a 6 and a 5. His partner rolls an 8, a 2 and a 3. James has won the haggle 2 times, while his partner only won 1 time. Thus, James is allowed to move the item value by one tier to get himself a higher profit. He chooses to push the price up in order to get to a higher commerce level. Unfortunately, the trader is unable to afford this new item value and thus is unable to complete the trade.

item.title: Adrenaline
item.description:
Throughout a game of 5D, player characters accumulate a unique resource known as Adrenaline. This resource can be spent to influence the narrative of the game in small but significant ways.Every player character has an Adrenaline level. This level sits between 0 and an upper limit, which is defined by the characters progression tier. Quickplay characters' Adrenaline level can be 3 at most. Only player controlled characters have an Adrenaline level. Adrenaline Levels reset to 0 after characters end a Respite or ReclusionAdrenaline can be accumulated throughout the game through different ways. Every player character gets one unique method of gathering Adrenaline, which is decided together with the narrator upon character creation. This method usually is unique to that character.Example Clio is a human mercenary. Her player and the narrator decided that her way of accumulating Adrenaline lies within being at the front line of a battle. Thus, the exact event that causes the character to gain adrenaline is decided to be whenever she manages to inflict a Wound or more severe injury to an opponent. Example Jason the lumberjack loves riddles, this is one of his characters minor quirks. He has a rather high intelligence for it and even a minor vocation set to solving puzzles. The narrator offers Jasons player the option to grant Adrenaline whenever Jason manages to come up with creative and unique solutions to a problem. A player may spend any amount of Adrenaline to create an Adrenaline Rush. The player must spent the Adrenaline cost, and pick one effect from this list of possible effects: Sheer Luck (costs 1 Adrenaline, as often as desired): The player may add 1 to a Skill Check before the roll to increase their odds of success.
Second Wind (costs 2 Adrenaline, once per Skill Check): The player may reroll a Skill Check with the same dice pool. The player has to use the new result. Flipping the Script (costs 2 Adrenaline, once per scene): The player may retroactively alter the scene in favor of their character in a minor way. The narrator has to approve this change, otherwise the player may choose something else. Back from the dead (costs 4, once per respite): The player, after losing all their Constitution or Resilience during a skirmish, may rise up one time to perform a single turn the next time their faction gets to act, after which the character can no longer continue onwards and has to totally recover. After an Adrenaline Rush the character can not experience another until they have taken a Rest. Example Clio the mercenary has accumulated her maximum of 3 Adrenaline. When she enters a dark cavern with no preparation, her player announces to spend 2 Adrenaline points in order to Flip the Script, which gives the player the option to add to the scene that Clio luckily has brought a stack of unused torches which she can light up. The remaining Adrenaline remains on the character sheet. Clio can not experience another Adrenaline Rush until she rests.

item.title: Downtime
item.description:
Whether it is a quiet nights rest, a week spent working for an inn, or a season of laying low after a brutal encounter, downtime is the easiest way characters may heal, grow, plan and advance long term tasks that can not be completed in short time frames. Downtime represents the low-stakes times between intense moments, where not much story relevant activity can be paragraphed, and instead a summary could be given.On a mechanical level, downtime can be used to recover constitution and resilience from being injured or stressed. On a narrative level, downtime can be used to further progress in quests, personal goals, tend to hobbies, or provide character or world building.Downtime can be taken by any character, in three different tiers: Rests, Respites and Reclusions. Depending on the chosen downtime period, each character may spend the time to perform some low-stakes activities, the boundaries of which are to be discussed with the narrator. Additionally, two passive boons are affecting the player automatically at the end of the downtime: Recovery: Players get to remove some amount of injuries and stress from their character sheet depending on their downtime period, regaining any distributed Vitality tokens not yet collected again: At the end of a scene, players get to remove one Agitation. At the end of a rest, players get to remove one Strain, plus all Agitation and one Panic At the end of a respite, players get to remove all Strain and one Wound, plus all Agitation and Panic and one Burnout. At the end of a reclusion, players get to remove all Strains and Wounds and one Trauma, plus any Stress. If a player decides to remove a trauma that was not treated before taking the reclusion, they permanently lose that Constitution slot. Refresh: Characters regain access to any spent uses of features that can only be activated a certain number of times per that downtime period. Rests last at least eight hours or an equivalent time frame, unless otherwise stated. A rest refers to a character taking a break from an intense activity to recover spent strength and unwind from intense moments. During a rest, a character may choose 2 activities.Activity examples: Sleeping: A character takes a nap for 6 hours. The player may choose 1 Strain to remove from their character sheet. This option can be picked multiple times to remove more Strains. Choosing this option a second time only increases the time slept to 8 hours instead of 12. Tending to a companion: A character with some medical vocation (or engineering vocation for mechanical beings) may tend to another injured character to apply medical treatment. The tending character rolls a Synergy check using their vocation. A simple success let's the attended player remove one Strain. A critical success let's the attended character remove all Strain they are equipped for. This action costs adequate medical supplies to perform, not having access to enough medical supplies will increase the difficulty of the Skill Check. Providing emotional support: A character may choose to offer support for another struggling character that has at least one Agitation. The supporting character rolls a Synergy check. On a success, the supported character may remove one Panic, on a critical success the supported character may remove all Panic. Additional activities: A character may choose to perform other light activity that is fitting for the narrative and the character. This can be highly character and story specific, thus it is crucial to work with the narrator to decide what activities are allowed to be performed Respites last at least a week or an equivalent time frame, unless otherwise stated. A respite refers to a character using multiple days to advance small goals, or to recover from a fierce fight. During a respite, a character may choose 1 activity per day in respite.Activity examples:
Gathering intel: A character spends the time to collect information about something in a low-risk environments. The narrator may have the player roll one or more mental skill checks depending on hours the character attempts to collect such information, and then attach the info received to the result of the Skill Check. The amount and quality of intel acquired depends on the number of days spent with this activity. Taking a side hustle: A character picks up a new job to earn a decent amount of money on the side. The actual amount depends on the type of job, the characters abilities and the number of days spent with this activity. This may affect the characters Vitality recovery. Errand running: A character runs a sequence of small errands to either earn a tiny amount of money, get information, explore their surroundings, or engage in trading. One errand sequence takes up one day. Acquiring new equipment: A character visits a store or shop to purchase or bargain for new equipment to take it with them on the next journey. This activity takes up a whole day, but may be combined with other shopping sprees into the same activity.
Tending to a companion: A character with some medical vocation (or engineering vocation for mechanical beings) may tend to another injured character to apply medical treatment. The tending character rolls a Synergy check using their vocation. A simple success let's the attended player remove one Wound. A critical success let's the attended character remove all Wounds they have, or mark a Trauma as treated. This action costs adequate medical supplies to perform, not having access to enough medical supplies will increase the difficulty of the Skill Check. Providing emotional support: A character may choose to offer support for another struggling character that has at least one Agitation. The supporting character rolls a Synergy check. On a success, the supported character may remove one Burnout, on a critical success the supported character may remove all Stress from their harm list. Any Rest Activity Reclusions last at least 2 months or an equivalent time frame, unless otherwise stated. A reclusion refers to a character laying low for a long time period without having anything with big stakes happen. During a respite, a character may use all passive boons and choose additionally 1 activity per 2 weeks or an equivalent time frame in reclusion.Activity examples: Respecialize: A player may spend any newly earned skill points. Additionally, a player may also repurpose one skill point from one skill to another, as long as the resulting skill points stay between -1 and 3. Training: A player may change their characters vocations any way they see fit that match the characters recent experiences.
Advance a long term goal: A character may take a lot of time to progress a personal quest or task by a massive amount, potentially providing some outcome to this task. See ../Proben > Progress Clocks for details. Business Building: A character may use their current vocations to start or expand a business for the time of the reclusion. The amount of money earned depends on the type and location of the business Any Respite Activity While characters are taking time off to recover, the world they inhabit does not stand still. Politics advance, cities bloom and blight, wars are fought, and every time characters decide to take downtime, they lose the ability to largely influence the status of the world.

item.title: Character Creation
item.description: A character within Mortal Engines: XXXXXX can be created within minutes of having a good idea. Using the following process you can manifest your dream easily.It all starts with a simple idea, known as the pitch. A pitch is a narrative hook that draws the attention of the audience into the pitched idea by providing an interesting twist to an otherwise potentially mundane thought. The pitch should be no longer than two lines of text and is written down as the very first thing of your character. Secondly, from this pitch can a concept be formed. The concept is more concrete than the pitch in terms of how that character fits into the world, but it is not as constricting as choosing a city to be born in. This concept is the backbone of your character design, and on a narrative level as many elements of the character creation process should conform to support this core idea.After finding a concept, it is time to fill in the backstory of the character. The backstory tells about a characters past, and the reasons why they are in their current position. It is recommended to keep the backstory simple and written in bullet points for easier reference.With the backstory you also know where the character stands in their own life. Now you can fill in the characters identity, which consists of their name, age, origin, and characteristics. The character origin refers to the place they were born. Characteristics mean special features of the character that are minor tweaks to the appearance or behavior that can be used to gain rust.After the backstory it is time for the goals and aspirations. You know where the character came from, now you need to decide on where they want to go. A character can have a single goal or multiple goals. It is recommended to keep the list of goals as short as possible. Goals represent achievements that are to the character worth pursuing for their intrinsic values rather for external motivation.A characters beliefs guide the character throughout the world with a filter of morale through which the character forms a certain world view, which in turn shapes their morals. From a characters origin their beliefs can be inferred, and the initial moral is copied from the kind of society they are a part of.After the narrative part of the character is created, the gameplay section can be filled in. Using the notes from the previously filled areas, decide on how to spend the characters skill points. On tier 1, a character has 2 skill points, and on tier 2 a character has 4 skill points. All core skills of any character start at 0 skill points, which translates into a D6. Skill points are used on skills to increase the dice value by 2 (to a maximum of 3 with a resulting D12), or a skill point may be gained by reducing one D6 to a D4.The second part of the abilities are the professions. Every character must additionally have at least a major profession and may have a minor profession at the cost of 1 skill point. The major profession is their way of earning their daily bread, be it by playing music for others, accounting finances, robbing people on the streets or hunting animals in the wild. The minor profession can be either a hobby, a side project that has nothing to do with the major profession, or it could even be a former job that still left some knowledge. Professions are used during skill checks where applicable major professions grant a highroll to the result and applicable minor professions add +2 to the result.A characters starting equipment heavily depends on a multitude of factors, like the origin, the characters upbringing, their backstory, the professions, or their morals. It is best practice to work with the game master on this point, as the starting equipment can vary wildly between characters.

item.title: Einführung und Spielvorbereitung
item.description:
Dieses Rollenspiel ist ein Setting-agnostisches kooperatives System zum Erzählen von Geschichten, welches von mindestens zwei Spielern gespielt wird. Dieses Regelwerk kann für eine Reihe verschiedenster Settings angepasst werden. Für eine Partie 5D treffen sich mehrere Spieler, um gemeinsam eine Geschichte innerhalb eines vordefinierten Settings zu erzählen, indem Rollenspielen als Haupttreiber von Entscheidungen der Charaktere, während Ausgänge von Aktionen mit unbekanntem Ergebnis in der Regel durch Würfelwürfe bestimmt werden. Ein Spieler, bekannt als der Erzähler, beschreibt die Welt der Geschichte und wichtige Elemente in der Umgebung der Spielercharaktere, welche von den restlichen Spielern kontrolliert und gesteuert werden. Jeder Spieler darf einen oder mehrere Charaktere übernehmen, während der Rest vom Erzähler als Nichtspielercharaktere dargestellt werden.
Jeder Spieler benötigt Zugriff zu zwei vollständigen Sets an Würfeln, u.A. einem D4, D6, D8, D10, D12, D20 and D%). Weiterhin braucht jeder Spieler einen Charakterbogen pro Charakter, welchen der Spieler übernimmt. Zu guter Letzt sollten alle Spieler die Regeln, welche für ihre Rolle relevant sind, gelesen und verstanden haben, inklusive Setting-spezifischer Regeln, die das Spielgeschehen beeinflussen können.
Setting-spezifische Vorbereitung Jedes Setting befolgt die Grundregeln, welche in diesem Dokument beschrieben werden, jedoch kann ein Setting weitere Regeln hinzufügen, welche die Grundregeln über neue Mechaniken oder Subsysteme erweitern, verändern, oder überschreiben. Sollte ein Setting mehr Vorbereitung als hier beschrieben benötigen, so beschreibt das Setting diese Vorbereitung selbst.
Nachdem alle Spieler sich auf eine bestimmte Art Geschichte einigen konnten, entscheiden alle gemeinsam, wer die Rolle des Erzählers übernimmt. Der Rest fährt als Spieler fort. Jeder Spieler übernimmt einen oder mehrere Charaktere innerhalb der Geschichte, um die Aktionen dieser Charaktere zu diktieren. Diese gewählten Charaktere heißen Spielercharaktere. Die Erzähler haben eine besondere Glossar > Rolle unter den Spielern: Sie sind dafür verantwortlich, die Umgebung der Spielercharakter und Auswirkungen der Handlungen dieser zu beschreiben. Prinzipiell sollte jeder Spielercharakter zu Beginn des Spiels ein Ziel haben, welches sie im Laufe des Spiels verfolgen können. Dieses Ziel erlaubt den Erzählern, im Vorfeld zu begreifen, wohin die Geschichte in diesem Spiel geführt werden soll.Sobald ein Erzähler bestimmt wurde, kann dieser die Spielvorbereitung beginnen.Spielvorbereitung Es ist hilfreich, als Erzähler sich ausreichend Zeit für die Vorbereitung des Spiels zu nehmen. Es kann (und wird) viel improvisiert werden, unvorbereitet sollte ein Erzähler jedoch nicht in das Spiel hineingehen.
Eine Partie 5D vorzubereiten ist ähnlich zum Ausarbeiten eines Handlungsstrangs in einer Geschichte. Geschichten sind typischerweise in einen oder mehreren Handlungsbögen strukturiert. Jeder dieser Handlungsbögen dann in Kapitel unterteilt werden, welche wiederum aus mehreren Szenen bestehen.
Wenn eine neue Partie 5D vorbereitet wird, kann der Erzähler ein Kapitelziel festlegen. Darüber hinaus muss nur die grobe Handlungsrichtung bekannt sein, in die der Erzähler die Geschichte tragen möchte, da in einer Partie selten mehr als ein Kapitel am Stück erzählt wird. Dieses Kapitelziel sollte sich zusammensetzen aus der aktuellen Narrative, dem derzeitigen Handlungsbogen, und den Zielen der Spielercharaktere. Das Ziel kann, falls vorhanden, auch ein Kapitelziel aus einer vergangenen Partie sein.
Nachdem das Kapitelziel grob bestimmt wurde, sollte der Erzähler nun die aktuelle Situation der Spielercharaktere beachten, wo sie sich gerade befinden und was sie zu tun planen. Ab diesem Zeitpunkt kann die Spielvorbereitung stark variieren. Sollten die Spieler eine vergangene Geschichte weitererzählen wollen, ist dem Erzähler geraten, diese vergangene Geschichte noch einmal aufzuarbeiten, und eventuelle lose Handlungsstränge wieder aufzugreifen. Dies nimmt eine Menge Vorbereitungsarbeit ab, da viele offene Fragen für die kommende Partie bereits geklärt sind, z.B. wie die Spielercharaktere sich getroffen haben, und was sie zum aktuellen Zeitpunkt erreichen wollen. Sollten die Spieler eine neue Geschichte erzählen wollen, müssen sie kollektiv diese Fragen selbst beantworten. Um eine neue Geschichte zu beginnen, kann es hilfreich sein, dieses Fragen zu beantworten: Wer ist jeder Spielercharakter? Was haben die Spielercharaktere gemeinsam? Was unterscheidet sie? Unter welchen Umständen haben die Charaktere jeweils erstmals interagiert? Das Kapitelziel sollte an dieser Stelle angepasst werden, dass die Informationen über die Spielercharaktere mit einfließen.
Der Erzähler ist dazu angehalten, verschiedene Optionen bereit zu halten, wie das Kapitelziel erreicht werden kann. Idealerweise decken die verschiedenen Optionen mehrere Spielstile ab, um den Spielern eine breite Auswahl an brauchbaren Auswahlmöglichkeiten zu bieten, von denen sie sich ihre jeweilig favorisierten Arten aussuchen können. Anschließend sollte der Erzähler eine Handvoll Kern-Szenen vorbereiten, welche die grobe Handlung über das nächste Kapitel darstellen und die verschiedenen Optionen ausbreiten.
Zu guter Letzt, sobald das Kapitelziel bekannt und der Weg dorthin grob markiert wurde, kann der Erzähler mit den Spielern eine eröffnende Szene bestimmen, welche optional mit beschreibt, wie die Charaktere sich zuerst getroffen haben, falls sie sich noch nicht kennen.5Dice ist dazu ausgelegt, als narratives Hilfsmittel eine Geschichte zu erzählen. 5Dice ist kein Kriegssimulator, und beschränkt die Anzahl an Würfelwürfen auf ein Minimum, ohne den Spiel-Teil des Rollenspiels komplett auszuhebeln. Wie jede Geschichte kann eine Narrative in 5Dice in Handlungsbögen unterteilt werden, welche aus Kapiteln bestehen, die wiederum aus Szenen bestehen. Jede dieser Geschichts-Einheiten kann so groß oder so klein sein, wie die Spieler es für sinnvoll ansehen. Ein ganzes Kapitel kann durch einen einzeln Satz übersprungen werden, und einzelne Szene kann über mehrere Stunden hinweg gezielt durchgespielt werden.

item.title: Equipment
item.description: During skirmishes, carrying weapons and wearing armor has oftentimes proven effective to amplify ones chances of success for reaching their goal, be it defeating other characters, invading a base, or simply surviving a battle. In 5D, weapons and armor are pieces of equipment that a character has in their inventory.Weapons are the main way of increasing both the reach of an attack damage output of said attack. To track weapons effectively and efficiently, they all fall into this scheme: Name: every weapon has an identifier. By default it is a descriptor of the weapon, eg "Boxing Gloxes", but players are free to rename weapons however they like, as long as it is clear what weapon they are talking about. Type (Skill): every weapon that is used to attack relies on some form of wielder skill. For melee weapons, this is usually Might, ranged weapons usually use Agility. Advanced weapons that don't require manual input typically require Wits, and magical weapons might rely on Charms. Reach: the effective range the weapon is meant to be used in. If the target is too close, the attack automatically stalls. If the target is too far and the weapon is a melee weapon, the attack can not hit. If the target is too far and the weapon is a ranged weapon, the attack automatically stalls. By default, there are 5 different ranges: Grapple, Melee, Long, Far, and Artillery. Miniature Fights only: Which range the distance between a character and the target falls under is determined by these measurement methods: If the distance between the two characters is less than the narrators pinky fingers width, the range is Grapple. If the distance between the two characters is less than the narrators index fingers and pinky fingers combined, the range is Melee. If the distance between the two characters is less than the distance from the narrators index pinky finger to the index finger, the range is Long. If the distance between the two characters is less than the narrators maximum span of a single hand, the range is Far. Anything outside that distance falls under Artillery. Grip: most weapons need to be held in some form. Every weapon lists what kind of grip is necessary to use it properly. Improper grip can be used, if narratively justifiable, but results in a stall. By default, there are 3 different grips: One Handed, Two Handed and Twinned. Damage Types: every attack uses some sort of damage type. Resistances and Vulnerabilities against these damage types change the injury severity on the target. By default, there are 3 different damage types: Blunt, Cut, Pierce Extra Traits: any important additional info about the weapon gets noted down here. Example on a Twinned Grip Sable: "Twin Blades: when used with a second Theft Blade and the character has a vocation for using weapons, attacks automatically surge". While weapons can prove useful to afflict stronger injuries on an opponent, armor helps protecting against too severe injuries. Armor comes in three coverage tiers, each protecting more than the previous one: half armor, when worn, will cover the bulk of the wearer. full armor, when worn, will cover the bulk and appendages of the wearer but decrease dodging by -2 closed armor is made up of full armor and an ace piece, which covers the wearers weak point but decreases dodging by -4. Along with these three cover tiers, armor comes in different strength levels: basic armor adds a static modifier of +2 for blocking. refined armor adds a static modifier of +4 for blocking. fortified armor adds a static modifier of +6 for blocking.

item.title: Aerial Cavalry
item.description: Witches in the military Bomber jacket, duh. Stays on you (because it's fastened), doesn't produce a lot of drag, and keeps you warm at high altitudes (because it was designed for that). Also give them a WW1 pilot hat for the same reason. ...OK, this is not even that stupid. A double-breasted, fur-lined leather jacket could be done with buckle or button-fastening instead of a zipper to fit a pre-industrial tech level. Make it black of course because tradition (and nocturnal camouflage), give them a tight hood, a pair of goggles, and a fancy scarf because why not, plus a pair of riding trousers. They could wear the pointy hat and a black wool cloak as their dress uniform. As an aesthetic recommendation inuit style snow goggles could be a cool eyewear choice, depending on how common glass is in your setting / how tech averse the witches are. As for issues you'd face going really quick (without magic protection), you get cold pretty easily even in relatively decent weather if you aren't wearing the wearing proper attire. (And using a vehicle that doesn't shield you in the least, such as a broom, as opposed to a motorcycle with fairings that guide the majority of the wind away from you) So while the bodysuit might help minimize air resistance and clothing flapping about, it might not be ideal for your witches not suffering hypothermia. Could serve as a bit of flavour, making cold climates a viable counter to witches, by significantly limiting their air time, unless they wear warm clothes, which minimize their speed... Then you could counter the counter, by having witches who specialized in just that situation and discovered magic that protects them from the cold while wearing their trademark skintight suits.

item.title: Dungeon
item.description: Die Spieler sind in einem klassischen Dungeon und sollen ein Artefakt beschaffenPeriphäre Systeme: Statuseffekte Umgebungsgefahren

item.title: Dämonenbeschwörung
item.description: Die Spieler sind Teenagerfreunde, die als Mutprobe einen Dämon beschwören wollen - leider beschwört das einen echten Dämon, der nicht freundlich gesinnt ist.Periphäre Systeme: Statuseffekte

item.title: Kampf in der Taverne
item.description: In einer Taverne sitzt die Gruppe Spieler, als plötzlich ein paar betrunkene Gäste anfangen die Spieler zu belästigenPeriphäre Systeme: Statuseffekte

item.title: Kollosseum
item.description: Die Spieler sind Krieger in einer Arena und sollen um die Gunst auf dem Schlachtfeld kämpfen.Periphäre Systeme: Battle Kits Massenunterhaltung

item.title: Marktplatzschlacht
item.description: Während eines gewöhnlichen Shoppingtrips bricht auf dem Markt plötzlich ein Tumult aus, als ein paar Diebe einen Händler ausrauben und davonrennen. Die Spieler werden in der Not aufgefordert, die Diebe zu stoppenPeriphäre Systeme: Improvisierte Waffen Umgebungsgefahren

item.title: Monsterjagd im Nebel
item.description: Die Gruppe Spieler sind Kryptologen, welche in einem Nebeligen Sumpf ein Monster suchen - nur um herauszufinden, dass es wirklich existiertPeriphäre Systeme: ???

item.title: Narrative - Mortal Engines
item.description: Mortal Engines is set in a post-apocalyptic world, where humanity has rebuilt its cities to traverse the unforgiving lands. No connecting roads between cities allow motorized vehicles to find their way around the outskirts, instead most intercity travel happens on lighter-than-air floating airships. The game of Mortal Engines: XXXXXX is about resourcefulness, clever solutioms to problems, and preplanning. This game throws the players into the world, with the game master acting as their five senses. They each embody one or more characters to roam the lands of the hunting grounds, the static settlements of the Anti-Traction-League, or any other place on earth, to trade goods with witting salespeople, to uncover dark secrets within conspiracies in the fight for survival between the cities. The game masters role is to provide an interactive world for the players that can be changed as they desire, they will take note of the players actions and let the world react accordingly. The players role is to be curious about the world and story the game master is presenting, and to interact with elements of the world in creative ways to find solutions to challenges the game master prepares for the player characters to overcome.

item.title: Schiffsgefecht
item.description: Die Spieler bemannen ein oder zwei kleine Schiffe, um ihren Heimathafen zu verteidigen.Periphäre Systeme: Größenskalierung von Verletzungen

item.title: Tanzbühne
item.description: Die Gruppe wird in Paare aufgeteilt, jedes Paar soll kooperativ in einem Tanzwettbewerb die meisten Punkte erzielen. Dann wird die Bühne gestürmt, doch dank der Security, welche das ganze kaschieren will, denkt die Menge, dass das zur Show gehört. Jetzt müssen die Spieler tanzend die Eindringlinge besiegen.Periphäre Systeme: Kooperative Angriffe Massenunterhaltung

item.title: Tempel
item.description: Die Spieler betreten einen Tempel, als plötzlich die dekorativen Statuen zum Leben erwachen.Periphäre Systeme: Umgebungsgefahren Fallen

item.title: Cantina Gilstando
item.description: In der Schule war sie stets Leistungsträgerin. Das führte sich im Studium fort. Jetzt ist sie angesehene Forscherin.Nobelpreis gewinnenWissen ist Macht, und Macht muss geteilt werden.Wissenschaftlerin . Adrenalin Level: 1 Maximales Adrenalin: 2Eine neue Entdeckung machenKomplimente erhalten $143.75 Italienerin, spricht mit starkem italienischem Akzent

item.title: Frederick 'Fallen Star' Falster
item.description: Frederick war ein begnadeter Sänger. Seine Stimme zeichnete sich bei seinen stinkreichen Eltern schon früh als besonderes Talent aus, weshalb sie ihn frühzeitig mit ihren finanziellen Mitteln förderten und forderten. Daraus wurde ein selbstverliebter Eigenbrödler mit Überlegenheitskomplexen, welcher mit seinem Charme vor niemandem Halt macht, um zu bekommen, was er will.Ewiger Ruhm und Reichtum auf der ganzen WeltIch bin der Geilste überhaupt und niemand kann mir irgendwas anhaben. Wenn mir jemand etwas will, erfährt mein Vater davon und sorgt dafür, dass er bestraft wird.Künstler-Multitalent Adrenalin Level: 1 Maximales Adrenalin: 2Applaudiert werden $32478.34 (Taschengeld von letzter Woche)

item.title: Jill Helberne
item.description: Jill ist eine absolute Sportskanone. Sprints, Hürdenlauf, Schwimmen, Bogenschießen, wenn es um Geschwindigkeit geht, ist sie die Beste. Ihr junges Leben war stark fokussiert auf körperliche Ertüchtigung, und sie will als gutes Vorbild gelten.Olympia-Gold erhaltenWer seinen Körper nicht pflegt, verdirbt ihn. Dass zu ändern sollte jeder in der Lage sein, und ich zeige, wie es geht.Athletin Adrenalin Level: 0 Maximales Adrenalin: 2Einen Wettkampf gewinnen $532.22

item.title: Ulricht Spiekenburg
