The backstory tells about a characters past, and the reasons why they are in their current position. It is recommended to keep the backstory simple and written in bullet points for easier reference.With the backstory you also know where the character stands in their own life. Now you can fill in the characters identity, which consists of their name, age, origin, and characteristics. The character origin refers to the place they were born. Characteristics mean special features of the character that are minor tweaks to the appearance or behavior that can be used to gain rust.After the backstory it is time for the goals and aspirations. You know where the character came from, now you need to decide on where they want to go. A character can have a single goal or multiple goals. It is recommended to keep the list of goals as short as possible. Goals represent achievements that are to the character worth pursuing for their intrinsic values rather for external motivation.A characters beliefs guide the character throughout the world with a filter of morale through which the character forms a certain world view, which in turn shapes their morals. From a characters origin their beliefs can be inferred, and the initial moral is copied from the kind of society they are a part of.After the narrative part of the character is created, the gameplay section can be filled in. Using the notes from the previously filled areas, decide on how to spend the characters skill points. On tier 1, a character has 2 skill points, and on tier 2 a character has 4 skill points. All core skills of any character start at 0 skill points, which translates into a D6. Skill points are used on skills to increase the dice value by 2 (to a maximum of 3 with a resulting D12), or a skill point may be gained by reducing one D6 to a D4.The second part of the abilities are the professions. Every character must additionally have at least a major profession and may have a minor profession at the cost of 1 skill point. The major profession is their way of earning their daily bread, be it by playing music for others, accounting finances, robbing people on the streets or hunting animals in the wild. The minor profession can be either a hobby, a side project that has nothing to do with the major profession, or it could even be a former job that still left some knowledge. Professions are used during skill checks where applicable major professions grant a highroll to the result and applicable minor professions add +2 to the result.A characters starting equipment heavily depends on a multitude of factors, like the origin, the characters upbringing, their backstory, the professions, or their morals. It is best practice to work with the game master on this point, as the starting equipment can vary wildly between characters.

item.title: Crafts
item.description: Characters are able to create new objects from available resources and materials. Crafting can be useful to invent new weapons on the fly, forge evidence or cook food. Even medical surgery can be tracked with this system.To begin crafting, characters need knowledge over what they are trying to craft. This can be almost anything that takes some time to create, be it a hearty meal, or writing a computer program, or composing a heartwarming melody specifically for an audience made up of nobles.When the product is clear, the narrator creates a progress clock that the crafting character has to complete. The number of segments represents the amount of work that goes into the crafting process, and the number of successes determines how well the character has to perform at the craft to succeed as a whole. While other progress clocks use a single threshold to determine success and failure for this task, crafting can take up to four different outcomes all depending on the values for these two variables. Successes on each segment are determined based on normal skill checks for this kind of task, eg Agility for making a weapon, Charms for painting a picture, Wits for analyzing a super complex virus. Characters are able to use approaches here if narratively justifiable.To perform crafts, characters have to invest time. This time is most often found in downtime, during which characters are free to do anything they wish to do as long as they are uninterrupted. The total time needed to finish a craft is the number of segments multiplied by the amount of time per segment. Characters are free to choose how much of their downtime they want to spend on the craft. Whenever the character performs the required skill check on the craft, the player fills the next segments in the progress clock based on the skill checks outcome: Critical failure adds two failures simple failure adds one failure complicated success does nothing and just loses time simple success adds one success critical success adds two successes. It is possible to help out other characters when they craft something. To help a crafting character, the character has to have knowledge over the goal. When that is given, the helping character can perform a Synergy approach with the crafts required skill. This mainly reduces the time it takes to complete a craft, but is an equally great opportunity to roleplay the characters interaction on a specific topic.After the craft has been finished, i.e. the progress clock is fully filled out, the completing player counts the number of successes on the progress clock. If the success threshold has been reached, the craft was successful and the character get exactly what they set out to do, otherwise it failed and they get some other thing. If the craft has been completed with at least three more successes than required, the craft was a critical success and the character gets an item that does not only do what they wanted it to, but the narrator adds some extra on top. If the craft has been completed with 3 or more successes less than required, the craft failed critically. Not only does the character not achieve what they intended to, but they also get some other heavy drawback on it, e.g. the materials are not recoverable, or the item destroys itself.

item.title: Travel
item.description: Whenever characters attempt to traverse a distance across a landscape that generally does not provide enough visual interest to warrant moment-to-moment roleplay, the narrator may choose to divert to the travel event system. This system offers a simplified way of handling travel by compressing the journey into a montage of events that players get to encounter and play out.To use this travel event system, the narrator needs to define the distance that is to be traversed and categorize it on three main distances: Near, Far and Very Far. The exact distance is not relevant to decide on the systemic distance, but they are exponentally incremental of another. The relative distance dictates how many events are going to be presented on this journey: Near is the shortest distance, only 1 event gets added to the journey. Far is the next longer distance, it has 2 events added to the journey. Very far is the farthest distance that can be traveled without any rest in between, 3 events get added to the journey in this case. The narrator should be aware of how far the group has traveled since the last rest to suggest when the party takes a rest for the day. Tip The party needing rest can be pinned on specific characters having a lower endurance or being of a lineage with below average physical abilities, but this option should be considered with a lot of care to not stir up conflicts between the players. Planning the journey Planning the journey could also be presented as an optimization problem, where the goal is to get to a specific location within a certain time frame while using up as little resources as possible. This could lead to characters researching possible paths and dangers along these, stocking up supplies and tools, and looking for options to potentially hitch a ride to boost their exploration speed. Example A group of adventurers starts off their journey in a small town in the east of the country. They find that their path leads them to the west, into the central capitol. The narrator grants the parties historian knowledge about their potential paths, one option leads through the swamp, where after an improvised camp they will be able to make a stop at a village if they manage to convince the locals they have no ill intentions. Another option has them pass through the mountains, where they will have no option to make a stop in an established settlement, but in turn they might be able to shave off a day of travel time. It is now up to the characters to debate their journey path and prepare for heading out. After the distance is defined, the narrator may add the number of events required by the journey length from the encounter types: The event types heavily depend on the tone of the campaign and the current narrative. Thus, the narrator is advised to handcraft these events like other encounters, and sequence them in a way that wont contradict the current narrative.Each of these events acts like a small stop between segments of uninterrupted motion. Both during and outside these events, the roleplay doesnt have to be sidelined, all players are welcome to roleplay as their characters to enhance the scene or provide character building. Just like the rest of the game, this is highly specific to the story and narrative at hand.

item.title: Downtime
item.description:
Whether it is a quiet nights rest, a week spent working for an inn, or a season of laying low after a brutal encounter, downtime is the easiest way characters may heal, grow, plan and advance long term tasks that can not be completed in short time frames. Downtime represents the low-stakes times between intense moments, where not much story relevant activity can be paragraphed, and instead a summary could be given.On a mechanical level, downtime can be used to recover constitution and resilience from being injured or stressed. On a narrative level, downtime can be used to further progress in quests, personal goals, tend to hobbies, or provide character or world building.Downtime can be taken by any character, in three different tiers: Rests, Respites and Reclusions. Depending on the chosen downtime period, each character may spend the time to perform some low-stakes activities, the boundaries of which are to be discussed with the narrator. Additionally, two passive boons are affecting the player automatically at the end of the downtime: Recovery: Players get to remove some amount of injuries and stress from their character sheet depending on their downtime period, regaining any distributed Vitality tokens not yet collected again: At the end of a scene, players get to remove one Agitation. At the end of a rest, players get to remove one Strain, plus all Agitation and one Panic At the end of a respite, players get to remove all Strain and one Wound, plus all Agitation and Panic and one Burnout. At the end of a reclusion, players get to remove all Strains and Wounds and one Trauma, plus any Stress. If a player decides to remove a trauma that was not treated before taking the reclusion, they permanently lose that Constitution slot. Refresh: Characters regain access to any spent uses of features that can only be activated a certain number of times per that downtime period. Rests last at least eight hours or an equivalent time frame, unless otherwise stated. A rest refers to a character taking a break from an intense activity to recover spent strength and unwind from intense moments. During a rest, a character may choose 2 activities.Activity examples: Sleeping: A character takes a nap for 6 hours. The player may choose 1 Strain to remove from their character sheet. This option can be picked multiple times to remove more Strains. Choosing this option a second time only increases the time slept to 8 hours instead of 12. Tending to a companion: A character with some medical vocation (or engineering vocation for mechanical beings) may tend to another injured character to apply medical treatment. The tending character rolls a Synergy check using their vocation. A simple success let's the attended player remove one Strain. A critical success let's the attended character remove all Strain they are equipped for. This action costs adequate medical supplies to perform, not having access to enough medical supplies will increase the difficulty of the Skill Check. Providing emotional support: A character may choose to offer support for another struggling character that has at least one Agitation. The supporting character rolls a Synergy check. On a success, the supported character may remove one Panic, on a critical success the supported character may remove all Panic. Additional activities: A character may choose to perform other light activity that is fitting for the narrative and the character. This can be highly character and story specific, thus it is crucial to work with the narrator to decide what activities are allowed to be performed Respites last at least a week or an equivalent time frame, unless otherwise stated. A respite refers to a character using multiple days to advance small goals, or to recover from a fierce fight. During a respite, a character may choose 1 activity per day in respite.Activity examples:
Gathering intel: A character spends the time to collect information about something in a low-risk environments. The narrator may have the player roll one or more mental skill checks depending on hours the character attempts to collect such information, and then attach the info received to the result of the Skill Check. The amount and quality of intel acquired depends on the number of days spent with this activity. Taking a side hustle: A character picks up a new job to earn a decent amount of money on the side. The actual amount depends on the type of job, the characters abilities and the number of days spent with this activity. This may affect the characters Vitality recovery. Errand running: A character runs a sequence of small errands to either earn a tiny amount of money, get information, explore their surroundings, or engage in trading. One errand sequence takes up one day. Acquiring new equipment: A character visits a store or shop to purchase or bargain for new equipment to take it with them on the next journey. This activity takes up a whole day, but may be combined with other shopping sprees into the same activity.
Tending to a companion: A character with some medical vocation (or engineering vocation for mechanical beings) may tend to another injured character to apply medical treatment. The tending character rolls a Synergy check using their vocation. A simple success let's the attended player remove one Wound. A critical success let's the attended character remove all Wounds they have, or mark a Trauma as treated. This action costs adequate medical supplies to perform, not having access to enough medical supplies will increase the difficulty of the Skill Check. Providing emotional support: A character may choose to offer support for another struggling character that has at least one Agitation. The supporting character rolls a Synergy check. On a success, the supported character may remove one Burnout, on a critical success the supported character may remove all Stress from their harm list. Any Rest Activity Reclusions last at least 2 months or an equivalent time frame, unless otherwise stated. A reclusion refers to a character laying low for a long time period without having anything with big stakes happen. During a respite, a character may use all passive boons and choose additionally 1 activity per 2 weeks or an equivalent time frame in reclusion.Activity examples: Respecialize: A player may spend any newly earned skill points. Additionally, a player may also repurpose one skill point from one skill to another, as long as the resulting skill points stay between -1 and 3. Training: A player may change their characters vocations any way they see fit that match the characters recent experiences.
Advance a long term goal: A character may take a lot of time to progress a personal quest or task by a massive amount, potentially providing some outcome to this task. See ../Skill Checks > Progress Clocks for details. Business Building: A character may use their current vocations to start or expand a business for the time of the reclusion. The amount of money earned depends on the type and location of the business Any Respite Activity While characters are taking time off to recover, the world they inhabit does not stand still. Politics advance, cities bloom and blight, wars are fought, and every time characters decide to take downtime, they lose the ability to largely influence the status of the world.

item.title: Commerce
item.description: During their ventures, characters sometimes need some form of currency to purchase certain items from other characters. These currencies are not tracked completely, but via a commerce system.Every character posesses a purse. In this purse, all their money is stored. A purse can hold any amount of money of any currency introduced. A purse is a physical item that can be stolen by others. The purse consists of a list of currencies with a number ranging from 0 to 8. This number indicates the characters relative wealth in that currency. Item prices are measured in this relative value as well.The relative values for characters are: 1: Pauper 2: Commoner, Paycheck-to-Paycheck-Liver 3: Tradesperson- Craftsperson 4: Accomplished Career, Small Business Owner 5: Wealthy, Successful Merchant, Minor Nobility 6: CEO, Nobility 7: Magnate, Tycoon, High Nobility 8: King / Queen, Head of Megacorporation Important It is important to note that these levels of commerce increase in wealth exponentially instead of linearly. Tip The commerce level of a character is only representing the amount of currency that player has on them, it does not include personal assets or wares to be sold. Examples for relative item values are: 0: A basic meal, worn down clothes, a beer, a magazine 1: Basic food for a day, a night at an inn, a new item of clothing, a bottle of high value alcoholics 2: An extravagant night out, a basic weapon, common livestock, full camping gear, simple transport 3: A well-crafted weapon, a decent horse/car/vehicle, simple armor, a small living space 4: A very fancy horse/car/vehicle, a nice home, an extravagant weapon, full plate armor, hired bodyguard 5: A mansion, luxury transport, estate, a personal guard or security forces, established business 6: A castle/skyscraper, enterprise level company, large army, a town, a significant military asset 7: A metropole, armada, small country 8: Kingdom, Nation Important Likewise with character commerce levels, item values increase exponentially instead of linearly. Example James is a well-off buseness man. He inherited a small fortune from his grandparents. In sum, this elevates his commerce level to tier 5. Should James acquire enough money to buy a new sportscar, this would only affect his commerce after a couple times over, due to exponential increases in wealth status. When a character decides to engage in trading, this usually results in either a bargain, where both parties agree on swapping assets with no money involved, or a trade. A trade is defined by one or more characters purchasing one or more items of some value from one or more other characters.The commerce levels of the buyer and seller are affected as follows: A character buying an item of a value higher than their own commerce level is not possible, they can not afford it. A character buying an item of a value equal to their own commerce level reduces their own commerce level by 2 tiers. A character buying an item of a value one tier less than their own commerce level reduces their own commerce level by 1 tier. A character buying an item of a value at least two tiers less than their own commerce level does not reduce their own commerce level. A character selling an item of a value higher than their own commerce level increases their own commerce level to that item values commerce level. A character selling an item of a value queal to their own commerce level increases their commerce level by 1 tier. A character selling an item of a value less than their own commerce level does not increase their own commerce level. Example James has wants to acquire the new sportscar he layed eyes on for a while now. His current commerce level of 5 matches the sportscars commerce value. On purchase James loses 2 commerce levels, bumping his fluid monetary value down to tier 3. This commerce level will take a while to recover to its normal level. Example James has has enough of his new sportscar. He wants to sell it. As he is currently commerce level 3, selling the sportscar will move it up to 5 again. When trying to make a deal with another character, a character can always try to increase their profits by haggling. This is resolved as three chained conflicts, with each character rolling a choice of INT or CHA each time. The character that rolled the higher result more gets to shift the items commerce value by one tier in a direction of their choosing.Example James tries to haggle during the sell of his sportscar. He rolls his charisma, which gives him a D10. His trade partner rolls intelligence, which gives them a D8. James rolls a 3, a 6 and a 5. His partner rolls an 8, a 2 and a 3. James has won the haggle 2 times, while his partner only won 1 time. Thus, James is allowed to move the item value by one tier to get himself a higher profit. He chooses to push the price up in order to get to a higher commerce level. Unfortunately, the trader is unable to afford this new item value and thus is unable to complete the trade.

item.title: Adrenaline
item.description:
Throughout a game of 5D, player characters accumulate a unique resource known as Adrenaline. This resource can be spent to influence the narrative of the game in small but significant ways.Every player character has an Adrenaline level. This level sits between 0 and an upper limit, which is the characters progression tier plus 1. Quickplay characters' Adrenaline level can be 3 at most. Only player controlled characters have an Adrenaline level. Adrenaline Levels reset to 0 after characters finish a Respite or ReclusionAdrenaline can be accumulated throughout the game through different ways. Every player character gets one unique method of gathering Adrenaline, which is decided together with the narrator upon character creation. This method usually is unique to that character.Example Clio is a human mercenary. Her player and the narrator decided that her way of accumulating Adrenaline lies within being at the front line of a battle. Thus, the exact event that causes the character to gain adrenaline is decided to be whenever she manages to inflict a Wound or more severe injury to an opponent. Example Jason the lumberjack loves riddles, this is one of his characters minor quirks. He has a rather high intelligence for it and even a minor vocation set to solving puzzles. The narrator offers Jasons player the option to grant Adrenaline whenever Jason manages to come up with creative and unique solutions to a problem. A player may spend any amount of Adrenaline to create an Adrenaline Rush. The player picks one effect from this list of possible effects and pays spends the required amount of Adrenaline: Sheer Luck (costs 1 Adrenaline, as often as desired): The player may add 1 to a Skill Check before the roll to increase their odds of success.
Second Wind (costs 2 Adrenaline, once per Skill Check): The player may reroll a Skill Check with the same dice pool. The player has to use the new result. Flipping the Script (costs 2 Adrenaline, once per scene): The player may retroactively alter the scene in favor of their character in a minor way. The narrator has to approve this change, otherwise the player may choose something else. Back from the dead (costs 4, once per reclusion): The player, after breaching their limits during a skirmish, may rise up once to perform a single turn the next time their faction gets to act with permanent Surge Momentum, after which the character can no longer continue onwards and has to totally recover - the character does not die through this option but has to take a reclusion immediately after using this ability. After an Adrenaline Rush the character can not experience another until they have taken a Rest. Example Clio the mercenary has accumulated her maximum of 3 Adrenaline. When she enters a dark cavern with no preparation, her player announces to spend 2 Adrenaline points in order to Flip the Script, which gives the player the option to add to the scene that Clio luckily has brought a stack of unused torches which she can light up. The remaining Adrenaline remains on the character sheet. Clio can not experience another Adrenaline Rush until she rests.

item.title: Vitality
item.description:
Severity tiers Incoming harm is measured in severity tiers, both for injuries and stress. For injuries, these severities are: Strain Wound Trauma For Stress, these severities are: Agitation Panic Burnout An injury on a leg could, depending on the severity tier, be anything from a scratch, to a broken bone, to a dismemberment. Vitality refers to a characters ability to withstand some amount of harm, both physically and mentally. Vitality is measured in constitution for physical harm and resilience for mental harm.When a character receives harm, be it stress or an injury, this has to be noted on the character sheet to track it over multiple scenes, chapters and in extreme cases even arcs and whole stories. This harm affects how much more a character can take. As long as these harms are not treated and recovered from, the character will be at higher risk of succumbing to a falling unconscious, mental collapseor dying outright. Vitality is tracked in two ways that play into each other: Through noting the different harms in a harm list, and through tracking active harm tokens. The number of Vitality tokens a character has is mostly determined through a characters lineage. This lineage lists 6 different token numbers, each depicting the number of tokens of one specific severity tier of either harm type. At the start of a new story, players each place the correct numbers of tokens according to their characters lineage on the character sheets Vitality area.Example An average human (lineage "Human") has the following Vitality values: 3 Strain 1 Wound 0 Trauma 3 Agitation 1 Panic 0 Burnout This makes any player with a human character gain 3 tier 1 injury tokens, 3 tier 1 stress tokens, 1 tier 2 token for both injuries and stress each, but no tier 3 tokens. Vitality tokens are used to mark fresh injuries and uncalmed stress on the character sheet. The details of this process of adding the harm to the character sheet and assigning a Vitality token is different between injuries and stress.Injuries can be obtained in two ways: By being hurt by other characters or by failing physical skill checks. When a character gets hurt by another character, the result of the attack is considered for deciding the initial severity, see Attack Actions for detail. On environmental damage, the severity depends on how hard the character fails their skill check.Tip Success with complications can give Strain injuries, but this should be used only if the narrator can not think of better complications. It is generally more fun to come up with narratively impactful consequences to a character barely making it. When a character receives an injury of a given severity, they go through the following process:Some characters withstand certain types of physical damage more easily or suffer types of physical damage more intensely. These interactions are called Resistances and Vulnerabilities respectively. When a character gets injured with a certain severity, the player checks to see if they have resistances or vulnerabilities against the incoming damage types. They sum up each of the interactions and subtract the number of resistances from the number of vulnerabilities, and then modify the severity of the incoming injury according to that number: If the number is negative, they reduce the severity by this amount of tiers. Should an injury by this process be reduced to less than a strain, it gets dropped. If the number is positive, they increase the severity by the number of tiers. Should an injury by this process be escalated to more than a Trauma, the character immediately enters the Dying state. Tip Incoming attacks should not have too many unique damage types to not bloat the process of checking resistances and vulnerabilities. Likewise, custom lineages should not have too many resistances and vulnerabilities as well for the same reason. Example Barry the athlete is currently competing in a race. He stumbled and fell, earning him an injury. Usually, this injury is a strain. The applied severity of this incoming injury depends on the damage type interactions. As Barry stumbles and falls to the ground, this is a scratch wound. Barry is not a human, however, but a character with a fictional lineage, which grant him resistance on bludgeoning damage due to their tough skin. The narrator allows the player to apply this resistance to the incoming damage. The injury gets reduced by one severity tier for each applicable resistance, which reduces the injury below a strain - letting the player ignore this injury outright. After applying resistances and vulnerabilities to an injury, and the injury severity exists somewhere from Strain to Trauma, the player writes down that injury with the given severity and the injury state ("A" for active) in the harm list on the character sheet. If the player can not add any more harm to the list due to it being full, the player may choose to have their character fall unconscious or enter the dying state.Next, the player removes one of their Constitution tokens of at least that injury severity to place on the newly added injury. If the player does not have a token of that severity, they look for a free token of the next higher severity and so on, until they either run out of tokens or can place one on the new injury. The player can at any time they need a Constitution token sacrifice a D8 on a physical skill to substitute a Strain token, a D10 on a physical skill to substitute a Wound token, or a D12 on a physical skill to substitute a Trauma token, as long as the player has 2 dice of that magnitude left in that skill. Should the player not have any options to place a token on an injury, the character enters the dying state.Should the player have sacrificed one of their D8s, D10s or D12s from a physical skill on an injury, they are unable to perform any skill checks with only that skill. Instead, they have to use an approach with another skill. Normal approach rules apply here.While injuries do not immediately affect the direct gameplay from moment to moment, they tend to affect the games upcoming scenes in a cascading way. It is thus very important to remove injuries as soon as possible so a character stays alive as long as possible.After an injury is added to the character sheet, it can be treated haphazardly. This is unique for every injury type, but usually involves some quickly improvised equipment, like a ripped apart shirt to function as makeshift bandages, or a pocket knife being repurposed into a terrible and unsanitary scalpel when no better cutting tools are available. When a player manages to have their character get one of their injuries treated, the player may move the Constitution token back to the Vitality graphic or reclaim the sacrificed dice into their dice pool, and change the injury state from "A" to "T" for "treated". This will not remove the injury outright, but enables a slow natural healing process for that injury. It also allows the player to roll raw skill checks with the skill they needed that dice for again.Injuries can be removed from the character sheet in two ways: Natural healing and medical treatment. Natural healing happens over time, while medical treatment can be done up to once per scene per character as long as the characters can afford it with adequate medical equipment and access to skill and knowledge in that area.Natural healing takes place during downtime:
At the end of a Rest, any resting player may remove 1 Strain injury from their harm list, giving back any not recollected Constitution tokens.
At the end of a Respite, they may remove all Strain and 1 Wound injury from their harm list, giving back any not recollected Constitution tokens. Trauma does not get removed through natural healing and always needs medical treatment to be cured. When a character seeks medical treatment, they need appropriate resources in the form of medical equipment, drugs and characters that are skilled in medical treatment of that kind. Once a character receives medical treatment for a specific injury, the player may take back any Constitution tokens and sacrificed dice from that injury immediately and change the state of that injury from "A" or "T" to "C" to remove it at the end of the next reclusion.Stress can be obtained in two ways: By being mentally hurt by other characters or by failing mental skill checks. When a character gets mentally hurt by another character, the result of the attack is considered for deciding the initial severity. On social damage, the severity depends on how hard the character fails their skill check.Tip Success with complications can give Agitation stress, but this should be used only if the narrator can not think of better complications. It is generally more fun to come up with narratively impactful consequences to a character barely making it. When a character receives stress of a given severity, they go through the following process:First, the player writes down the type of stress their character received with the severity tier. Next, the player removes one of their Resilience tokens of at least that stress severity to place on the newly added stress. If the player does not have a token of that severity, they look for a free token of the next higher severity and so on, until they either run out of tokens or can place one on the new stress. Should the player not have any free Resilience tokens of at least that severity left, they may sacrifice a D8 on a mental skill as an Agitation token, a D10 on a mental skill as a Panic token, or a D12 on a mental skill as a Burnout token. If that is also not possible anymore, the character enters the dying state.Should the player have placed one of their D8s, D10s or D12s from a mental skill on any stress, they are unable to complete any raw skill checks with only that skill. Instead, they have to use an approach with another skill. Normal approach rules apply here.Unlike injuries, stress can not be treated through medical care. Instead, it takes time spent in safety and among good company to heal stress. Outside of skirmishes, other characters may approach a stressed out character and provide some peptalk. This action results in a raw Synergy skill check of the approaching character. The outcome of that skill check depends on the result of that skill check:Other than having people to talk to, holding downtime in a safe area also decreases stress over time: At the end of a rest the player may remove 1 Agitation. At the end of a respite, the player may remove all Agitations and 1 Panic. At the end of a reclusion, the player may remove all stress from the characters resilience. Should a character suffer too much harm at once, it leads to breaching limits. Characters have three limits that can be breached: running out of injury tokens breaches the constitution running out of stress tokens breaches the resilience running out of space on the harm list breaches the endurance limit. Each of these limit breaches lead to a default outcome for the player, but if narratively justifiable, the player may take a different option. The default options for each of these cases are: Breaching consitution The character gains a final surge, a last stand during which nothing will stand in their way. For 5 seconds or 1 faction turn, any skill checks the character would have performed are considered a critical success. After this timespan the character dies irrevocably and is removed from the story permanently. Breaching resilience The characters grip on reality slips. From now on, the character gets 6 final actions. Each time the character performs an action, the player rolls a D6. If that die roll is higher than the number of remaining actions, the character fully lost their mind and becomes unresponsive to outside influences. Breaching endurance The character has endured too much over a long period of time, they are unable to continue on like this, the body automatically takes emergency measures. The player picks one of their character's most severe harms in the endurance list. This harm now becomes unhealable and the state gets changed to "P" for permanent. If a token is placed on the harm, it gets voided. The character stays unresponsive for the remainder of the scene, but does not get removed from the story. After the scene ends, the player regains control over the character.

item.title: Glossary
item.description:
An umbrella-term for a number of unique active behaviors that directly influence the current Environment and Scene.
Multiple of these form a or story. Can typically be divided into multiple Chapters.
A special form of rolling a Skill Check, where the Character swaps out one of the required Skill Dice with another they might be better at, as long as the Player can justify this switch narratively.
Multiple of these form a narrative Arc. Can typically be divided into multiple Scenes.
A partaker within the Narrative of the game, who is able to interact with their surroundings through Actions.
A circumstance that alone might be easy to deal with, but rises the tension of the Narrative in the short term.A polymetrically shaped body with differently marked faces.A die with 4 consecutively numbered sides, starting at 1.A die with 6 consecutively numbered sides, starting at 1.A die with 8 consecutively numbered sides, starting at 1.A die with 10 consecutively numbered sides, starting at 1.A die with 12 consecutively numbered sides, starting at 1.A die with 20 consecutively numbered sides, starting at 1.
A D10 but the numbers are multiplied by 10 each.
Multiple Dice together create a dice pool, which can be used to give more control over a Dice Roll than a single die.
The act of using one or more dice of certain Magnitudes to determine a randomly generated number called the Result.
A numerical value depicting how hard a task is for an average person of a certain Lineage is to complete.
A physical harm that is inflicted upon a Character. Comes in a severity tier and has to be healed to recover from.
A group of offsprings from a common progenitor, who are usually identified by their Mechanical traits.The number of sides of a die. The commonly used ones are the D4, D6, D8, D10, D12 and D20.A set of rules that link together to form a unit of gameplay.
See either Static Modifier or Momentum.
See either Surge Momentum or Stall Momentum.The overarching path through a series of events that occur within the game.
The player who describes the World and has it react to the Players actions.
The immediate change in the Scene after a Dice Roll turned out a certain Result.A person playing the game.
A character that is controlled by a Player who is not the Narrator.
The number of a Dice Roll, after adding applicable Modifiers.
A Result of a Skill Check where the sum of the rolled numbers including all modifiers is between 7 and 9.
A Result of a Skill Check where the sum of the rolled numbers including all modifiers is 3 or less.
A Result of a Skill Check where the sum of the rolled numbers including all modifiers is 15 or above.
A Result of a Skill Check where the sum of the rolled numbers including all modifiers is between 4 and 6.
A Result of a Skill Check where the sum of the rolled numbers including all modifiers is between 10 and 15.
The job of a Player within a game. Typically is either Narrator or Player.The act of embodying a character, fictional or otherwise, through manner of talking, describing their actions, or simply reacting in specific ways.
A circular disk divided into segments that track results of actions for an abstract goal, where Characters can cooperate on gathering enough successes to accomplish the Clock and each segment takes up a certain time frame.
The smallest unit of a depicted narrative. Multiple of these form a Chapter.
A specific time and geographic location within a narrative, either non-fiction or fiction. Can be used interchangeably with World.
A relative numerical value indicating the training of a Character within a certain domain of Actions. Skills can have values from -1 to 3.
The ability of a Character to be charming, charismatic, or to read the intentions of others.
The ability of a Character to perform dextrous tasks that require nimbleness.
The ability of a Character to help and support others. Raw power.
The ability of a Character to comprehend complex topics, deduce facts, inventing new tools, or holding general knowledge.
The act of rolling one or two Dice according the the tasks Difficulty, applying any relevant Modifiers and comparing the Result number to generate an Outcome.
Requires a Skill and a Difficulty to be performed by a Player.The numeric named value a characters skill is at. The 5 skill levels are Inept, Novice, Journeyman, Expert, and Master.
Allows increase of a Skill level of a Character to increase the likelyhood of success on tasks using this Skill.
Performing a Dice Roll twice and taking the lower Result.
A number that gets added to the Result of a Dice Roll, creating a new Result. Each Static Modifier can only be added once per Roll.
Performing a Dice Roll twice and taking the higher Result.
The heart of any game of 5D. Typically split into multiple Arcs.
A set of closely related Mechanics that link together to form a part of a playable game.
A set of Subsystems and Mechanics that link together at different interfacing areas to create a complete game.The location a specific game takes place in.

item.title: Tatsächlicher Aufbau der Lokomotive
Skizzierter Plan der Lokomotive
Der Entlaufene Zug Schienennetz Karte
item.guid: 5Dice/Abenteuer/Der entlaufene Zug/Media/Der Entlaufene Zug Schienennetz Karte.jpg

item.title: Skill Checks
item.description:
Skill Checks are a tool to determine the Outcome of any interaction with not predetermined Results. They can be used whenever a Character attempts something and the Outcome is not fully known.
A Skill Check always follows a simple structure: First, the Narrator determines the Skill to be used and the Difficulty of the task. Then, the Player rolls the required Dice and sums up the Result, using any applicable Modifiers. Finally, the Narrator and the Player work together to interpret the Result into an Outcome that fits within the Narrative of the game.
In order for a Character to see how well they do on a given Skill Check, the Narrator has to set the Difficulty. This Difficulty is a numeric value ranging from 0 to 5 that increases linearly with rising difficulty of the task. The name of that numerical value indicates the intended Difficulty of that Skill Check for an average Character of that Lineage within the Setting.
Example Barbara wants to pick a lock. Because the lock is quite old and posesses a rather simple mechanism, the Narrator sets this Skill Check as an Uncommon Grace task.
Example Grimsby is trying to jump up a tall ledge. The narrator sets the Skill Check as a Challenging Might task. Since Grimsby is a kangaroo and not a human, he gets to reduce the Difficulty by 1 tier for jumping related actions as part of his Lineage. Thus, Grimsby has to succeed at an Uncommon task rather than a Challenging one to succeed in this Action.
Once the Narrator announces the Skill used for the task, the Player can pick up the required dice to roll in the check depending on the Characters training level in that Skill:
Example Barbara tries to pick the lock. Her Grace is at +1, giving her access to a 2D8 Dice Pool.
Example Grimsby has 2 Skill Points in Might, allowing him to roll 2D10 on his attempt to jump up the ledge.
Depending on the task at hand, the Difficulty can vary. Oftentimes, the task does not exactly match the Characters Skill level perfectly, which offsets the two values.
Depending on the constellation of these two variable tiers, different Skill Check Modifiers apply automatically:
Apart from outright failing or succeeding a task, two more types of Modifiers exist that can change the Outcome of a Skill Check: Momentum and Static Modifiers.
Momentum
Rolling with Momentum means rolling the same Skill Check twice, and taking the higher or lower Result, depending on whether the Character is Surging (higher result) or Stalling (lower result).
A Character is Surging once at least one active effect grants them Surge, but no Stalling effect is applied.
Likewise, a Character is Stalling once at least one active effect applies Stall to them, but no Surging effect is granted.
Should a Character have any combination of at least one Surging and at least one Stalling effect applied to them, they cancel each other out and the Character does not gain any of these effects.
Static Modifiers
Static Modifiers are a way to adjust the Result of a roll and adding a small number to it, be it positive or negative.
After the Dice Roll (including a potential Momentum roll) is made, the player adds all applicable Static Modifiers to the rolled number, forming a final Result.
Example Barbara wishes to pick a lock with a simple mechanism. The Narrator decided that this is an uncommon task. Barbaras Skill Level is 1, which matches the task Difficulty. She thus has to roll the check once.
Example Barbara tries to pick an old lock using her lockpicking equipment. This picklock grants her a simple +1 on lockpicking Actions. Barbara uses the 2D8 from her Skill and rolls a 10. The picklocks Static Modifier boosts her result to 11.
Example Grimsby the kangaroo wants to jump up a tall ledge. The Narrator set this as an challenging task, but Grimsby has a trait that lets him reduce the Difficulty of jumping tasks by 1, pushing it down to to uncommon Difficulty. Grimsbys Might is 2, meaning it is 1 higher than the Difficulty of the task. This grants Grimsby Surge Momentum when attempting to perform this Action.
Example Grimsby is attempting to jump up a ledge. Before jumping off, he takes a running start. The Narrator rules that this gives Grimsby a Surge Momentum, but because he already has that effect applied through performing an Action of 1 less Difficulty, this second Surge gets discarded. His first roll from 2D10 is a 5, which is not enough to succeed. His second Roll results in a 7. Not much better, but enough to succeed at the task.
Looking up the Result in the table above allows for interpretation of the Outcome. In this step, the Player and Narrator have to cooperate to tell a fitting and narratively satisfying Outcome for this task.
Example Barbara rolled an 11 when trying to lockpick an old lock, resulting in a Simple Success. Barbara is able to pick the lock easily without any guard nearby being alerted.
Example Grimsby achieved a 7 when trying to jump up the ledge. This barely reaches the a Complicated Success. The Player offers to take an Injury as Complication from misjudging the jump. The Glossary > Narrator describes how Grimsby takes a running start, jumps off and is able to reach the ledges height, but he jumped too early and now hangs on the edge of the ledge with his paws. The hard jolt on his arms strains the muscles and bones, and Grimsby has received a Strain through this Glossary > Action.
When a Character is faced with a challenge they are not likely to succeed at, the Player may decide to utilize a fitting Approach. Approaches are a way to boost a Character chances of success against a Glossary > Skill Check that otherwise would result in failure. The Approach does need to be approved by the narrator, as nothing narratively nonsensical should be incorporated into the Story.
When a new Approach is created, it is noted down on the Character Sheet. Upon first use, due to being something the Character is not yet trained in, this Approach can be used only with a Static Modifier of -2. This modifier does not apply on any subsequent uses of this Approach.
Example A rogue named Syla needs to convince a guard that they were not the one who tried to pickpocket them. The Narrator tells the Player controlling Syla that this is a challenging Charms check. As Syla is terrible in their persuasion, the player asks the Narrator if they could try to distract the guard using their Grace Skill, by throwing a rock against a nearby object unseen as if another Character just ran away. The Narrator allows this creative thinking as an Approach, as this still needs some convincing for the guard to believe there was another person there. So, Sylas Player now may use one Charms die and one Grace die in order to roll this Action. Syla has amazing Grace, granting the Player a D12 instead of the second D4 from a raw Charms check. As this is a new Approach, the Player makes this check but has to roll with a -2 Static Modifier. The two Dice result in a 11 and a 4, which sums up to 15. Subtracting the -2 gives a 13, which is enough for a Simple Success. Syla manages to distract the guard and convince them of there being another person, which gives them a tiny opportunity to flee from the scene.
It is possible to help out other Characters through the use of a Link Approach. The helping character rolls and Approach with the Link Skill and the Skill Checks required Skill with the Difficulty being 1 less than the original Skill Checks. The Outcome is a Static Modifier on the original Skill Check , whose value is determined through this table:
When a longer process gets started by one or more Characters, the Narrator may introduce a Progress Clock. These devices are a visual tool for showing long term progress towards some abstract goal.
Progress Clocks are usually divided into a number of segments that get filled as Actions related to that Clocks goal succeed or fail. Anytime a segment gets filled with a positive or negative value depending on success or failure, the narrator can have a positive or negative effect occur that will influence the current situation.
Most Progress Clocks follow this format:
An overall goal: This is the name of the Progress Clock. It informs about the target being pursued with this Clock. A number of segments: This is the number of actions that have to be performed at most in relation to the goal. At latest when the last segment gets filled in, the overall success on the goal should be evaluated. Time per segment: This number tells how long completing a single segment takes for a single character.
A number of successes: This is the threshold of success, at which the goal is generally considered to have a positive turnout. The Characters working on this Progress Clocks must succeed at least this many times to win this Progress Clocks.
A number of failures: This is the threshold of failure, at which the goal is generally considered to have a negative turnout. The Characters working on this Progress Clocks must not reach this number of failed actions on the goal to avoid the negative consequences. The number of successes and number of failures sum up to the number of segments plus 1.
Creating a Progress Clock A typical Progress Clock could look like this: Decypher the encoded letter: 8*1h - 6S/3F
This Progress Clock defines the overall goal to be the decoding of a script. The Clock has 8 segments and the timeframe for each segment is 1 hour, meaning this task will take 8 hours in total to complete. The number of successes is 6 and the number of failures is 3, this means that the characters must achieve at least 6 successes before they reach 3 failures in order to succeed at decyphering this letter.
For a Character to advance an existing Progress Clock, the character must perform an Action whose outcome aligns with the Clocks goal. When the Character does that, they roll a normal Skill Check to see whether they advance the goal or not: On a simple success or failure, the result is entered into the clock and the clock advances by one segment. Critical result variants double the number of successes/failures written on the clock and advance the clock by double the amount.
It is possible for a Character to help out another Character that is currently working on a Progress Clock by rolling a Link Approach with the substituting Skill being the one used by the Character directly working on the Progress Clock. Should the help consist of less direct actions, for example gathering appropriate materials, an appropriate Skill is to be used instead.
Once the worked on Clock has been filled out completely, the task is completed. How well the Characters did on the task and whether the goal has been accomplished depends on the numbers of successes and failures the Characters have added to the Clock:
If the number of required successes has been reached before the number of failures was, the Characters are successful in their task.
If the number of maximum failures has been reached before the number of successes was, the Characters fail in their task.
If the number of required successes has been surpassed byat least half of the number of failures (rounded down), the Characters succeed critically.
If the number of maximum failures has been surpassed by at least half of the number of successes (rounded down), the Characters fail critically.
Succeeding a Progress Clock The party is tasked with decyphering an encrypted letter. The task can be worked on in 1-hour segments and will take about 8 hours of work. The smartest two Characters share the workload and help each other out to advance the goal faster. After just 4 hours of their combined effort they are able to share their results with the rest of the party. The segments of the Progress Clock are filled in as follows: S-S-S-F-S-S-S-S. This sums up to 1 failure and 7 successes. As they have reached the required number of successes, they do succeed at the task and are able to read the decyphered letter. Even better yet, because they surpassed the number of successes by half of the number of failures rounded down (3/2 = 1.5 -> 1), they succeed critically, thus are able to extract more info out of the letter than expected.
Failing a Progress Clock The party is tasked with decyphering an encrypted letter. The task can be worked on in 1-hour segments and will take about 8 hours of work. The smartest two Characters share the workload and help each other out to advance the goal faster. After just 4 hours of their combined effort they are able to share their results with the rest of the party. The segments of the Progress Clock are filled in as follows: F-F-S-S-F-F-S-F. This sums up to 3 successes and 5 failures. As they have reached the maximum number of failures, they fail at the task and are not able to read the decyphered letter. Even worse yet, because they surpassed the number of failures by half of the number of successes rounded down (6/2 = 3), they fail critically. The party not only does not get the correct letter content, they manage to somehow misinterpret the whole letter and assume different information based on the wrong results.
